data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] Solitaire flow restoration device versus the Merci Retriever in patients with ac (The Lancet, 2012) ---
TREVO 2 Trial (PMID 22932714) - Trevo versus Merci retrievers for thrombectomy

ABSTRACT:
Background: Mechanical thrombectomy devices fail to achieve recanalization in 20-40% of large vessel occlusion strokes. This trial compared the newer Trevo Retriever against the FDA-cleared Merci Retriever.

Methods: This open-label randomized controlled trial enrolled 178 patients (ages 18-85) across 27 sites with angiographically confirmed large vessel occlusions and NIHSS scores of 8-29 within 8 hours of symptom onset. Patients were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive either Trevo or Merci thrombectomy. The primary efficacy endpoint was "thrombolysis in cerebral infarction (TICI) scores of 2 or greater reperfusion with the assigned device alone." Safety outcomes measured procedure-related adverse events.

RESULTS:
Efficacy: 86% of Trevo patients (76/88) achieved the primary endpoint versus 60% of Merci patients (54/90), representing a statistically significant superiority (odds ratio 4.22, 95% CI 1.92-9.69; p<0.0001)

Safety: Adverse events occurred in 15% of Trevo patients (13/88) compared to 23% of Merci patients (21/90), with no significant difference between groups (p=0.1826)

Interpretation: The authors concluded that Trevo Retriever should be preferred over Merci for treating patients with large vessel occlusion strokes who are ineligible for or unresponsive to intravenous thrombolysis.

--- [2] Systematic Evaluation of Patients Treated With Neurothrombectomy Devices for Acu (Stroke, 2018) ---
The STRATIS registry (Systematic Evaluation of Patients Treated With Neurothrombectomy Devices for Acute Ischemic Stroke) was designed to prospectively enroll patients treated in the United States with a Solitaire Revascularization Device and Mindframe Capture Low Profile Revascularization Device within 8 hours from symptom onset. A total of 984 patients treated at 55 sites were analyzed. The primary results were published in Stroke in October 2017. Mechanical thrombectomy with stent retrievers has become standard of care for treatment of acute ischemic stroke patients because of large vessel occlusion, demonstrating process timelines, technical, and functional outcomes comparable to large randomized trials in a real world cohort.

--- [3] Impact of Balloon Guide Catheter Use on Clinical and Angiographic Outcomes in th (Stroke, 2019) ---
PMID 30776994 - STRATIS BGC (Impact of Balloon Guide Catheter Use)

ABSTRACT/RESULTS:
This multicenter study examined how balloon guide catheters (BGC) affected outcomes in acute ischemic stroke patients undergoing mechanical thrombectomy. The research analyzed 745 patients across three catheter types.

KEY FINDINGS:
First-pass recanalization: 48% with BGC versus 26% with conventional catheters
Functional independence at 3 months: 61% achieved good outcomes with BGC compared to 42% with conventional approach
Modified first-pass effect: 66% success rate for BGC versus 42% for standard catheters

Clinical Significance: According to the abstract, "BGC use was an independent predictor of FPE, modified FPE, and functional independence," suggesting routine adoption may enhance early revascularization success and patient recovery rates.

Study Design: STRATIS was a prospective, multicenter registry with independent core laboratory review blinded to clinical outcomes. It specifically evaluated the Solitaire stent retriever as first-line therapy for large vessel occlusions.

--- [4] Impact of Stent Retriever Size on Clinical and Angiographic Outcomes in the STRA (Stroke, 2019) ---
PMID 30626287 - STRATIS Size (Impact of Stent Retriever Size)

ABSTRACT:
This retrospective analysis examined how stent retriever size affects outcomes in acute ischemic stroke patients. The study involved 715 patients treated with Solitaire stent retrievers in three size configurations: 4×20 (28%), 4×40 (38%), and 6×30 (34%).

Primary endpoints assessed:
- First-pass effect (FPE): "achieving near-complete revascularization (modified Thrombolysis in Cerebral Infarction ≥2c) after the first pass"
- Functional independence at 90 days using modified Rankin Scale (0-2)

RESULTS:
The 4×40 stent retriever demonstrated superior early revascularization:
- Highest FPE rates compared to 6×30 (p=0.003) and modified FPE rates superior to both 4×20 and 6×30 groups
- "Use of the longer stent retriever (4×40) was an independent predictor of achieving modified FPE"
- Final revascularization, functional dependence, and 90-day mortality showed no significant differences across size groups

Conclusion: The longer 4×40 stent retriever appeared most effective for achieving rapid clot removal, potentially improving early treatment success in stroke thrombectomy procedures.

--- [5] Middle Cerebral Artery M2 Thrombectomy in the STRATIS Registry (Stroke, 2021) ---
PMID 34311566 - STRATIS M2 (Middle Cerebral Artery M2 Thrombectomy)

ABSTRACT/OVERVIEW:
This observational study examined mechanical thrombectomy outcomes for middle cerebral artery M2 versus M1 occlusions using data from the STRATIS Registry, which tracked 984 acute ischemic stroke patients treated within 8 hours of symptom onset.

Patient Groups:
- M1 occlusions: 538 patients (54.7%)
- M2 occlusions: 170 patients (17.3%)

KEY FINDINGS:
Primary Results: M2 and M1 patients achieved comparable functional outcomes. Specifically, "rates of good functional outcome...and mortality...were similar between the 2 groups" at approximately 58-59% good outcomes and 14-15% mortality rates.

Reperfusion Success: Both groups demonstrated similarly high reperfusion rates, with "rates of successful reperfusion...similar between the groups (91% versus 95%)."

Safety Concern: "M2 patients had significantly increased rates of symptomatic ICH at 24 hours (4% versus 1%)," indicating a higher intracranial hemorrhage risk in the M2 group.

Clinical Implications: The authors concluded that while M2 thrombectomy produced comparable functional outcomes to M1 treatment, the increased hemorrhage risk warrants further investigation into whether mechanical thrombectomy benefits M2 occlusion patients.

--- [6] Variation in Vessel Size and Angiographic Outcomes Following Stent-Retriever Thr (Stroke: Vascular and Interventional Neurology, 2024) ---
DOI 10.1161/SVIN.123.000978 - STRATIS Vessel Size: Redirects to AHA Journals, access denied (403 error).

--- [7] Stent-Retriever Thrombectomy after Intravenous t-PA vs. t-PA Alone in Stroke (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
During a 2-year period at four centers in Catalonia, Spain, 206 patients who could be treated within 8 hours after the onset of symptoms of acute ischemic stroke were randomly assigned to receive either medical therapy (including intravenous alteplase when eligible) and endovascular therapy with the Solitaire stent retriever (thrombectomy group) or medical therapy alone (control group). All patients had confirmed proximal anterior circulation occlusion and the absence of a large infarct on neuroimaging. The primary outcome was the severity of global disability at 90 days. Among patients with anterior circulation stroke who could be treated within 8 hours after symptom onset, stent retriever thrombectomy reduced the severity of post-stroke disability and increased the rate of functional independence (43.7% versus 28.2% in controls). Symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage occurred at 1.9% in both groups.

--- [8] A Randomized Trial of Intraarterial Treatment for Acute Ischemic Stroke (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
HERMES Meta-Analysis - Background: The study pooled data from five 2015 randomized trials examining endovascular thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke caused by proximal anterior circulation artery occlusion. Methods: Researchers from the HERMES collaboration analyzed individual patient data from five trials (MR CLEAN, ESCAPE, REVASCAT, SWIFT PRIME, and EXTEND IA) conducted between December 2010 and December 2014. The primary outcome measured disability using the modified Rankin Scale at 90 days. Results: Endovascular thrombectomy produced significantly improved outcomes, with adjusted common odds ratio 2.49 (95% confidence interval 1.76-3.53; p<0.0001) compared to standard care. The number needed to treat was 2.6 patients to achieve one-level disability improvement for a single patient. Population Analyzed: 1,287 patients total (634 thrombectomy, 653 control). Subgroup Efficacy: Treatment benefits appeared consistent across diverse populations, with no heterogeneity of treatment effect across prespecified subgroups (p interaction=0.43). Notable effectiveness included elderly patients 80+ years and those treated beyond 300 minutes post-symptom onset. Safety: Mortality, parenchymal hematoma, and symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage rates did not differ significantly between groups. Conclusion: The analysis demonstrated endovascular thrombectomy benefits most stroke patients with large-vessel occlusion, regardless of demographics or geography.

--- [9] Randomized Assessment of Rapid Endovascular Treatment of Ischemic Stroke (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
SWIFT Trial - Background: The Solitaire Flow Restoration Device is a novel, self-expanding stent retriever designed to yield rapid flow restoration in acute cerebral ischaemia. Researchers compared this device against the Merci Retrieval System, the established mechanical thrombectomy standard. Methods: This randomized, parallel-group, non-inferiority trial enrolled 113 patients across 18 sites (17 USA, 1 France). Participants had acute ischemic stroke with moderate-to-severe deficits, treatable within 8 hours. The primary endpoint measured TIMI scale 2-3 blood flow without symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage after up to three device passes. Results: Primary Efficacy Outcome - Solitaire: 61% achieved target flow; Merci: 24% achieved target flow; Difference 37% [95% CI 19-53], odds ratio 4.87. 3-Month Neurological Outcomes - Solitaire: 58% showed good outcomes; Merci: 33% showed good outcomes; Difference 25% [6-43], odds ratio 2.78. Mortality at 90 Days - Solitaire: 17%; Merci: 38%; Difference -21% [-39 to -3], odds ratio 0.34. The trial demonstrated Solitaire's superior angiographic, safety, and clinical performance, leading to early termination via prespecified efficacy stopping rule.

--- [10] Endovascular Therapy for Ischemic Stroke with Perfusion-Imaging Selection (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
Background: The researchers investigated whether advanced imaging selection, newer devices, and early treatment could improve outcomes in endovascular stroke therapy, as prior trials showed variable results. Methods: Seventy patients with ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours of onset received either endovascular thrombectomy with a Solitaire FR stent retriever or alteplase alone. All participants had "occlusion of the internal carotid or middle cerebral artery" with salvageable brain tissue confirmed on CT perfusion imaging. Results: The trial stopped early due to efficacy. Endovascular therapy achieved superior reperfusion (median 100% vs. 37%, p<0.001) and improved early neurologic recovery at three days (80% versus 37%). Importantly, "more patients achieving functional independence (score of 0 to 2 on the modified Rankin scale, 71% vs. 40%; P=0.01)" at 90 days. No significant differences emerged in mortality or symptomatic bleeding rates. Conclusions: For patients with proximal arterial occlusion and salvageable tissue on imaging, early thrombectomy combined with initial alteplase "improved reperfusion, early neurologic recovery, and functional outcome" compared to alteplase monotherapy.

--- [11] Thrombectomy within 8 Hours after Symptom Onset in Ischemic Stroke (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
Background: The study tested whether advanced imaging selection, newer devices, and earlier intervention improve outcomes in endovascular stroke therapy. Methods: Researchers randomly assigned patients with ischemic stroke (within 4.5 hours of onset, receiving alteplase) to either endovascular thrombectomy with Solitaire FR stent retriever or alteplase alone. All participants had proximal arterial occlusion with salvageable tissue and ischemic core <70 ml on CT perfusion imaging. Key Outcomes: The trial stopped early after 70 patients due to efficacy. The endovascular group showed superior reperfusion at 24 hours (100% vs. 37%), greater early neurologic improvement at 3 days (80% vs. 37%), and better functional independence at 90 days (71% vs. 40%). There were no significant differences in rates of death or symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage. Conclusion: Early thrombectomy combined with alteplase, guided by CT perfusion imaging selection, significantly improved reperfusion, neurologic recovery, and functional outcomes compared to alteplase monotherapy in patients with proximal cerebral artery occlusion.

--- [12] Endovascular thrombectomy after large-vessel ischaemic stroke: a meta-analysis o (The Lancet, 2016) ---
The HERMES collaboration pooled individual patient data from five randomized trials (MR CLEAN, ESCAPE, REVASCAT, SWIFT PRIME, and EXTEND IA) conducted between December 2010 and December 2014. The analysis included 1,287 patients (634 assigned to endovascular thrombectomy, 653 assigned to control) with acute ischemic stroke caused by occlusion of the proximal anterior circulation artery. Thrombectomy significantly reduced disability compared to control (adjusted odds ratio 2.49, p<0.0001). The number needed to treat was 2.6. Benefits persisted across subgroups, including patients aged 80+, those treated >300 minutes after symptom onset, and those ineligible for alteplase. Mortality and hemorrhage rates showed no significant differences. Endovascular thrombectomy is of benefit to most patients with acute ischemic stroke caused by occlusion of the proximal anterior circulation.

--- [13] Safety and Efficacy of Solitaire Stent Thrombectomy: Individual Patient Data Met (Stroke, 2016) ---
The SEER meta-analysis combined individual patient data from four randomized trials (SWIFT PRIME, ESCAPE, EXTEND-IA, and REVASCAT) where Solitaire was the primary or only device used. The analysis involved 787 patients total: 401 receiving endovascular thrombectomy and 386 receiving standard care. The treatment demonstrated substantial benefit with a common odds ratio of 2.7 for improved outcomes. Approximately 40 of 100 treated patients experienced reduced disability, with 23 achieving independent functional status. The number needed to treat was 2.5 patients to improve disability by one level. Successful revascularization occurred in 77% of Solitaire-treated patients. No significant differences emerged in symptomatic hemorrhage or mortality rates between groups. Benefits remained consistent across all patient subgroups tested. Solitaire thrombectomy proved safe and highly effective with substantially reduced disability for large vessel ischemic stroke.

--- [14] North American SOLITAIRE Stent-Retriever Acute Stroke Registry (Stroke, 2013) ---
An investigator-initiated NASA registry recruited 24 clinical sites within North America to submit demographic, clinical, site-adjudicated angiographic, and clinical outcome data on consecutive patients treated with the Solitaire Flow Restoration device. The study examined 354 patients treated at 24 centers between March 2012 and February 2013. Recanalization success (TIMI ≥2) was achieved in 83.3% in NASA versus 83% in SWIFT. Modified Rankin Scale ≤2 at 90 days was observed in 42% of NASA patients versus 37% (SWIFT) and 40% (TREVO 2). 90-day mortality was 30.2% in NASA versus 17.2% (SWIFT) and 29% (TREVO 2). The investigators determined that the Solitaire FR device performance in clinical practice is comparable with the SWIFT and TREVO 2 trial results, demonstrating the device's effectiveness outside controlled trial settings.

--- [15] Solitaire flow restoration device versus the Merci Retriever in patients with ac (International Journal of Stroke, 2012) ---
This randomized non-inferiority trial enrolled 113 patients across 18 centers (primarily in the USA) with acute ischemic stroke requiring treatment within 8 hours. Patients received either Solitaire or Merci thrombectomy devices. The Solitaire group demonstrated superior performance: 61% achieved the primary efficacy endpoint versus 24% in the Merci group (odds ratio 4.87). Three-month neurological outcomes favored Solitaire (58% vs 33%), and mortality was lower with Solitaire (17% vs 38%). The Solitaire Flow Restoration Device achieved substantially better angiographic, safety, and clinical outcomes than did the Merci Retrieval System.

--- [16] Mechanical thrombectomy in acute stroke: prospective pilot trial of the solitair (American Journal of Neuroradiology, 2012) ---
This prospective, single-center trial evaluated mechanical thrombectomy feasibility, safety, and efficacy in patients with acute ischemic stroke using the Solitaire FR device under conscious sedation. Patients with large artery occlusions were treated within 6 hours (anterior circulation) or 24 hours (posterior circulation) of symptom onset. Among 36 patients treated between May 2010 and July 2011, successful revascularization occurred in 28 patients (77.8%). At 3 months, 22 patients (61.1%) achieved good functional outcomes (modified Rankin Scale ≤2). The procedure was technically feasible in 86.1% of cases, though the overall mortality rate reached 22.2%. Mechanical thrombectomy while under conscious sedation is feasible in a large percentage of cases and demonstrates favorable functional outcomes.

--- [17] Solitaire FR thrombectomy system: immediate results in 56 consecutive acute isch (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2018) ---
This study treated 56 consecutive acute stroke patients using intra-arterial therapy with the Solitaire FR system. The device successfully achieved recanalization in 50 of 56 patients (89%) with adequate TICI scores. Complications occurred in 5 patients, including two asymptomatic subarachnoid hemorrhages, two thromboembolic events, and one symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage. At discharge, 53.5% showed significant neurological improvement, and 46% achieved favorable functional outcomes. Solitaire FR is successful in achieving a high rate of arterial recanalization with a low complication rate, positioning it as an effective and safe thrombectomy option.

--- [18] Double solitaire mechanical thrombectomy in acute stroke: effective rescue strat (American Journal of Neuroradiology, 2014) ---
The Penumbra Pivotal Stroke Trial was a prospective, multicenter, single-arm study assessing safety and effectiveness of the Penumbra System for treating acute ischemic stroke caused by intracranial large vessel occlusive disease. The trial enrolled 125 patients with significant neurological deficits presenting within 8 hours of symptom onset. Participants had angiographically confirmed vessel occlusions and were either ineligible for or resistant to clot-dissolving medication. Successful revascularization occurred in approximately 82% of treated vessels. The procedure generated 18 adverse events in 16 patients (12.8%), with 3 serious events. Imaging revealed brain bleeding in 28% of patients within 24 hours, with symptomatic cases in 11.2%. Mortality reached 32.8% at 90 days, though 25% achieved favorable functional outcomes. The Penumbra System demonstrated safe and effective revascularization for stroke patients with large vessel blockages treated within the 8-hour window.

--- [19] Mechanical thrombectomy with the Solitaire AB device in acute ischemic stroke of (Neurology Journal, 2016) ---
The ASTER trial was a randomized, open-label, blinded end-point clinical trial conducted in 8 comprehensive stroke centers in France comparing contact aspiration versus stent retriever techniques for treating acute ischemic stroke with large vessel occlusion. Patients who presented with acute ischemic stroke and a large vessel occlusion in the anterior circulation within 6 hours of symptom onset were included. Patients were randomly assigned to first-line contact aspiration (n = 192) or first-line stent retriever (n = 189) immediately prior to mechanical thrombectomy. The primary outcome was the proportion of patients with successful revascularization defined as a modified Thrombolysis in Cerebral Infarction score of 2b or 3 at the end of all endovascular procedures. Among 381 randomized patients (mean age 69.9 years), 363 completed the trial. Successful revascularization occurred in 85.4% of the aspiration group versus 83.1% of the stent retriever group (P=.53). First-line thrombectomy with contact aspiration compared with stent retriever did not result in an increased successful revascularization rate at the end of the procedure, suggesting comparable effectiveness between the two techniques.

--- [20] Impact of age and baseline NIHSS scores on clinical outcomes in the mechanical t (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2014) ---
The COMPASS trial was a multicentre randomized trial evaluating whether direct aspiration as first pass treatment was non-inferior to stent retriever therapy for acute ischemic stroke from large-vessel occlusion. This trial enrolled 270 patients at 15 sites (10 US hospitals, 4 specialty clinics, 1 Canadian hospital). Participants had anterior circulation large-vessel occlusion within 6 hours of symptom onset. Patients were randomly assigned to either direct aspiration first pass or stent retriever first line thrombectomy. The primary outcome measured functional recovery at 90 days using the modified Rankin Scale (0-2 score), with a non-inferiority margin of 0.15. Aspiration achieved a modified Rankin score of 0-2 in 52% of patients versus 50% in the stent retriever group, demonstrating non-inferiority. Intracranial hemorrhage occurred in 36% of aspiration patients and 34% of stent retriever patients. Three-month mortality was 22% in both groups. The results support the use of direct aspiration as an alternative to stent retriever as first-line therapy for stroke thrombectomy.

--- [21] Intracranial thrombectomy using the Solitaire stent: a historical vignette (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2012) ---
A 67-year-old woman presented 3.5 hours after stroke onset with a right middle cerebral artery occlusion. Initial mechanical thrombectomy with a microbrush failed to achieve recanalization. The medical team then deployed a Solitaire stent within the blocked vessel. After several minutes of incubation, the expanded stent was slowly withdrawn under continuous aspiration with instantaneous removal of the entire thrombus and complete recanalization of the right MCA. The patient achieved a National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale score of 0 at 30-day follow-up, indicating complete recovery. Although initially designed for aneurysm treatment, the Solitaire stent proved safe and effective for thrombectomy, representing the first successful human clinical use of a Solitaire stent for this purpose and the ignition spark for the development of a whole generation of new devices, now called stent retrievers.

--- [22] Mechanical thrombectomy using a Solitaire stent retriever in the treatment of pe (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2019) ---
A prospective, single-center pilot study evaluated the safety and effectiveness of a self-expanding and fully retrievable stent (Solitaire AB) in revascularization of patients with acute ischemic stroke. The study included 20 patients with acute ischemic stroke attributable to a large artery occlusion of the anterior circulation within the first 8 hours from symptoms onset. Successful revascularization defined as thrombosis in cerebral ischemia grade 2b or 3 was achieved in 18 of 20 (90%) treated vessels, and 16 patients showed immediate restoration of flow after stent deployment. The average number of passes for maximal recanalization was 1.4, and the median time from groin puncture to recanalization was 50 (38-71) minutes. The Solitaire AB device can rapidly, safely, and effectively retrieve clots from major brain arteries within the treatment window.

--- [23] Solitaire stentectomy: 'deploy and engage' and 'loop and snare' techniques (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2016) ---
This article discusses mechanical thrombectomy using Solitaire FR stents for acute ischemic stroke caused by large vessel blockage. A significant complication is stent detachment during the procedure, which is associated with poor technical and clinical outcome. The paper presents two patient cases where conventional snare retrieval failed, prompting alternative techniques: the deploy and engage method and the loop and snare technique. Both approaches proved successful in removing the detached stents without requiring medications that could worsen acute stroke outcomes. The key advantage of retrieval techniques is the lack of need to administer fibrinolytic/antiplatelet agents that can potentially be catastrophic in the setting of acute stroke.

--- [24] Prospective, Multi-Centre, Single-Arm Study of Mechanical Thrombectomy using Sol (Stroke, 2013) ---
STAR was an international, multicenter, prospective, single-arm study of Solitaire FR thrombectomy in patients with large vessel anterior circulation strokes. A total of 202 patients were enrolled across 14 comprehensive stroke centers. Successful revascularization was achieved in 79.2% of patients. Favorable neurological outcomes occurred in 57.9% of participants, with device-related severe complications in 7.4% of cases. Mortality reached 6.9%, while intracranial bleeding occurred in 18.8% of patients, with only 1.5% being symptomatic. Solitaire FR treatment results in high rates of revascularization, low risk of clinically relevant procedural complications, and good clinical outcomes in combination with low mortality at 90 days.

--- [25] Technical Feasibility and Application of Mechanical Thrombectomy with the Solita (American Journal of Neuroradiology, 2013) ---
This study evaluated 14 consecutive patients with basilar artery occlusion (BAO) treated using the Solitaire FR Revascularization Device, often combined with thromboaspiration, thrombolysis, or additional stent placement. Overall, successful recanalization (TICI 3 or 2b) was achieved in all patients (TICI 3 in 78.6%, 11/14). Average device passes were 1.3, and median procedure time was 47 minutes. No device-related complications or symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage occurred. Good functional outcome (modified Rankin Scale 0-2) was achieved in 28.6% of patients. The multimodal endovascular approach demonstrated high recanalization rates with minimal complications, though underlying atherosclerotic lesions sometimes required permanent stent placement for complete vessel opening.

--- [26] Complications of Endovascular Treatment for Acute Stroke in the SWIFT Trial with (American Journal of Neuroradiology, 2013) ---
Twenty-three centers enrolled 634 consecutive patients treated between March 2013 and August 2015 using the Trevo device in the TRACK registry. Mean age was 66.1±14.8 years and mean baseline NIHSS score was 17.4±6.7; 86.7% had anterior circulation occlusions. The primary outcome was achieving a Thrombolysis in Cerebral Infarction (TICI) score of ≥2b. Among the 634 patients, 80.3% achieved successful revascularization. Forty-seven percent achieved favorable 90-day outcomes. Within guideline-recommended parameters (anterior circulation, 6-hour window), outcomes reached 51.4%. Mortality was 19.8%. The TRACK Registry validates that recent thrombectomy trial benefits translate to real-world practice, with treatment outcomes showing no significant differences between guideline-adherent and non-adherent cases.

--- [27] The INSPIRE Registry: Entering a New Era of Medical Device Research in the Neuro (Clinical Neuroradiology, 2020) ---
The Innovative Neurovascular Product Surveillance Registry (INSPIRE) is a unique, large scale, multitherapy, long-term observational study platform, sponsored by Medtronic Neurovascular, designed to continuously monitor the safety and performance of newly commercialized devices in real-world clinical settings. The first phase focuses on flow diverter devices (Pipeline Flex and Shield) with over 700 patients enrolled across 30 international sites. A second phase is enrolling stroke patients treated with Solitaire X and React 68/71 devices. Patients are prospectively enrolled from selected international sites whenever treated with the sponsor's new neurovascular devices, with all study data based on routine standard of care. The registry represents a collaborative approach between industry and the medical community, aligning with European medical device regulations regarding postmarketing surveillance.

--- [28] Solitaire Flow Restoration thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke: retrospective (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2013) ---
STORM-PE CAVT mechanical thrombectomy trial: This trial evaluated computer-assisted vacuum thrombectomy (CAVT) combined with anticoagulation versus anticoagulation alone for intermediate-high risk pulmonary embolism. The study enrolled 100 normotensive patients with acute PE (symptoms ≤14 days), elevated RV/LV ratios on imaging, and elevated cardiac biomarkers across 22 international sites. Results: The CAVT group demonstrated superior improvement in RV/LV ratio reduction (0.52±0.37) compared to anticoagulation alone (0.24±0.40), with a mean difference of 0.27 (95% CI, 0.12-0.43; P<0.001). Modified Miller scores showed greater improvements with CAVT versus anticoagulation alone at 48 hours (P<0.001). Early normalization of vital signs within 48 hours occurred more frequently following CAVT treatment. Major adverse event rates at 7 days were comparable between groups: CAVT 4.3% versus anticoagulation 7.5% (P=0.681). However, two PE-related deaths occurred in the CAVT arm.

data.Press Release Title: Medtronic Announces Early Site Release of New Radial Artery Access Portfolio Following First-in-Patient Procedure
data.Press Release Content: Medtronic's Radial Artery Access Portfolio. Device Details: Medtronic acquired two catheters from RIST Neurovascular designed specifically for neurointerventional procedures via radial artery access: Rist 079 Radial Access Guide Catheter—the first catheter specifically designed for neurointerventions using the radial artery pathway in the wrist, and Rist Radial Access Selective Catheter—supports device introduction and diagnostic agent delivery in neurovascular applications. Clinical Application: These devices target neurovascular conditions including brain aneurysms and other interventional neurovascular therapies. The radial approach offers advantages over traditional femoral access, citing lower bleeding and vascular complications per American Heart Association recommendations since 2018. Launch Information: Both devices received FDA 510(k) clearance in 2020. By February 2021, Medtronic completed over 80 cases across 12 institutions during market evaluation. RIST Neurovascular was acquired in August 2020 as part of Medtronic's tuck-in acquisition strategy. Expected to be immaterial initially but accretive long-term.

data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] Rist Guide Catheter for Endovascular Procedures: Initial Case Series from a Sing (Neurointervention, 2023) ---
This retrospective review of prospectively collected data identified 78 patients (mean age 60.3 years; 57.7% male) who underwent procedures using the Rist catheter. A high success rate of 98.7% was achieved, with radial artery access used in 91% of cases. Common procedures included coiling or stent-assisted coiling (16.7%) and angioplasty and stenting (16.7%). Five cases (6.4%) required crossover to femoral access, with limitations involving anatomical anomalies and unfavorable vessel geometry. The Rist catheter system demonstrated strong performance across various procedures, supporting its utility for neurointerventional work via transradial access.

--- [2] REACT Aspiration Catheters: Clinical Experience and Technical Considerations (Neurointervention, 2022) ---
This review examined REACT aspiration catheters used in mechanical thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke. REACT catheters feature a PTFE liner, nitinol reinforcement, polymer jacket, and hydrophilic coating optimizing navigation and aspiration performance. Technical factors affecting performance include the catheter's inner diameter, tip flexibility, catheter-to-vessel ratio, vacuum pressure, aspiration modes, and proximal flow arrest. Three studies involving 299 patients demonstrated successful recanalization in 89-96% of cases, with no procedural complications attributed to the device, and 24-36% achieving functional independence at 90 days. Navigation of REACT catheters is independent through favorable anatomy, though challenging locations may require stentriever anchoring or delivery assist catheter support. Both experimental data and clinical evidence support the safety and effectiveness of the REACT catheters.

--- [3] Initial experience with React 68 aspiration catheter (Interventional Neuroradiology, 2020) ---
This observational study tracked consecutive stroke patients treated with the React 68 catheter over seven months at a comprehensive stroke center, with 47 patients treated. Among 47 patients treated, Modified TICI 2b-3 was achieved in 45 of the 47 patients using aspiration alone or combined with stent retriever techniques across various vessel locations. Performance metrics were comparable between the React 68 and ACE 68 catheters. The React 68 represents a large-bore reperfusion catheter with trackability suitable for use in direct aspiration for recanalization of emergent large vessel occlusion.

--- [4] The INSPIRE Registry: Entering a New Era of Medical Device Research in the Neuro (Clinical Neuroradiology, 2020) ---
The Innovative Neurovascular Product Surveillance Registry (INSPIRE) is a unique, large scale, multitherapy, long-term observational study platform, sponsored by Medtronic Neurovascular, designed to continuously monitor the safety and performance of newly commercialized devices in real-world clinical settings. The first phase focuses on flow diverter devices (Pipeline Flex and Shield) with over 700 patients enrolled across 30 international sites. A second phase is enrolling stroke patients treated with Solitaire X and React 68/71 devices. Patients are prospectively enrolled from selected international sites whenever treated with the sponsor's new neurovascular devices, with all study data based on routine standard of care. The registry represents a collaborative approach between industry and the medical community, aligning with European medical device regulations regarding postmarketing surveillance.

--- [5] Bigger is Still Better: A Step Forward in Reperfusion With React 71 (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2020) ---
Penumbra Coil 400 safety and efficacy in small aneurysms: No PubMed article found matching this exact search.

data.Press Release Title: ESCAPE and EXTEND-IA Trials Confirm Benefits of Solitaire Flow Restoration Device for Stroke — Both Published in NEJM at ISC 2015
data.Press Release Content: ESCAPE and EXTEND-IA Trial Results Summary. Trial Overview: Both trials evaluated Medtronic's Solitaire device combined with IV-tPA for acute ischemic stroke treatment. Results were presented at the International Stroke Conference in Nashville and published in The New England Journal of Medicine. Clinical Outcomes - ESCAPE Trial (316 patients): Functional independence improved significantly at 53% vs. 29% with IV-tPA alone, stroke mortality reduced by approximately half at 10% vs. 19%. EXTEND-IA Trial (70 patients): Functional independence rate of 71% vs. 40% with IV-tPA alone, hospital/rehabilitation time reduced dramatically from 73 to 15 days, recanalization rates at 24 hours of 100% with Solitaire vs. 37% with IV-tPA alone. Key Clinical Significance: According to Dr. Bruce Campbell, the landmark results indicate that a profound change in how we provide stroke care is needed. The trials demonstrated that stent thrombectomy addresses cases where patients miss the treatment window or have blood clots too large for pharmaceutical dissolution. Regulatory Impact: Both studies were halted early by safety committees due to compelling results, indicating strong clinical benefit. Findings aligned with earlier MR CLEAN trial outcomes, establishing broader evidence supporting endovascular treatment guidelines.

data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] Solitaire flow restoration device versus the Merci Retriever in patients with ac (The Lancet, 2012) ---
TREVO 2 Trial (PMID 22932714) - Trevo versus Merci retrievers for thrombectomy

ABSTRACT:
Background: Mechanical thrombectomy devices fail to achieve recanalization in 20-40% of large vessel occlusion strokes. This trial compared the newer Trevo Retriever against the FDA-cleared Merci Retriever.

Methods: This open-label randomized controlled trial enrolled 178 patients (ages 18-85) across 27 sites with angiographically confirmed large vessel occlusions and NIHSS scores of 8-29 within 8 hours of symptom onset. Patients were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive either Trevo or Merci thrombectomy. The primary efficacy endpoint was "thrombolysis in cerebral infarction (TICI) scores of 2 or greater reperfusion with the assigned device alone." Safety outcomes measured procedure-related adverse events.

RESULTS:
Efficacy: 86% of Trevo patients (76/88) achieved the primary endpoint versus 60% of Merci patients (54/90), representing a statistically significant superiority (odds ratio 4.22, 95% CI 1.92-9.69; p<0.0001)

Safety: Adverse events occurred in 15% of Trevo patients (13/88) compared to 23% of Merci patients (21/90), with no significant difference between groups (p=0.1826)

Interpretation: The authors concluded that Trevo Retriever should be preferred over Merci for treating patients with large vessel occlusion strokes who are ineligible for or unresponsive to intravenous thrombolysis.

--- [2] Systematic Evaluation of Patients Treated With Neurothrombectomy Devices for Acu (Stroke, 2018) ---
The STRATIS registry (Systematic Evaluation of Patients Treated With Neurothrombectomy Devices for Acute Ischemic Stroke) was designed to prospectively enroll patients treated in the United States with a Solitaire Revascularization Device and Mindframe Capture Low Profile Revascularization Device within 8 hours from symptom onset. A total of 984 patients treated at 55 sites were analyzed. The primary results were published in Stroke in October 2017. Mechanical thrombectomy with stent retrievers has become standard of care for treatment of acute ischemic stroke patients because of large vessel occlusion, demonstrating process timelines, technical, and functional outcomes comparable to large randomized trials in a real world cohort.

--- [3] Impact of Balloon Guide Catheter Use on Clinical and Angiographic Outcomes in th (Stroke, 2019) ---
PMID 30776994 - STRATIS BGC (Impact of Balloon Guide Catheter Use)

ABSTRACT/RESULTS:
This multicenter study examined how balloon guide catheters (BGC) affected outcomes in acute ischemic stroke patients undergoing mechanical thrombectomy. The research analyzed 745 patients across three catheter types.

KEY FINDINGS:
First-pass recanalization: 48% with BGC versus 26% with conventional catheters
Functional independence at 3 months: 61% achieved good outcomes with BGC compared to 42% with conventional approach
Modified first-pass effect: 66% success rate for BGC versus 42% for standard catheters

Clinical Significance: According to the abstract, "BGC use was an independent predictor of FPE, modified FPE, and functional independence," suggesting routine adoption may enhance early revascularization success and patient recovery rates.

Study Design: STRATIS was a prospective, multicenter registry with independent core laboratory review blinded to clinical outcomes. It specifically evaluated the Solitaire stent retriever as first-line therapy for large vessel occlusions.

--- [4] Impact of Stent Retriever Size on Clinical and Angiographic Outcomes in the STRA (Stroke, 2019) ---
PMID 30626287 - STRATIS Size (Impact of Stent Retriever Size)

ABSTRACT:
This retrospective analysis examined how stent retriever size affects outcomes in acute ischemic stroke patients. The study involved 715 patients treated with Solitaire stent retrievers in three size configurations: 4×20 (28%), 4×40 (38%), and 6×30 (34%).

Primary endpoints assessed:
- First-pass effect (FPE): "achieving near-complete revascularization (modified Thrombolysis in Cerebral Infarction ≥2c) after the first pass"
- Functional independence at 90 days using modified Rankin Scale (0-2)

RESULTS:
The 4×40 stent retriever demonstrated superior early revascularization:
- Highest FPE rates compared to 6×30 (p=0.003) and modified FPE rates superior to both 4×20 and 6×30 groups
- "Use of the longer stent retriever (4×40) was an independent predictor of achieving modified FPE"
- Final revascularization, functional dependence, and 90-day mortality showed no significant differences across size groups

Conclusion: The longer 4×40 stent retriever appeared most effective for achieving rapid clot removal, potentially improving early treatment success in stroke thrombectomy procedures.

--- [5] Middle Cerebral Artery M2 Thrombectomy in the STRATIS Registry (Stroke, 2021) ---
PMID 34311566 - STRATIS M2 (Middle Cerebral Artery M2 Thrombectomy)

ABSTRACT/OVERVIEW:
This observational study examined mechanical thrombectomy outcomes for middle cerebral artery M2 versus M1 occlusions using data from the STRATIS Registry, which tracked 984 acute ischemic stroke patients treated within 8 hours of symptom onset.

Patient Groups:
- M1 occlusions: 538 patients (54.7%)
- M2 occlusions: 170 patients (17.3%)

KEY FINDINGS:
Primary Results: M2 and M1 patients achieved comparable functional outcomes. Specifically, "rates of good functional outcome...and mortality...were similar between the 2 groups" at approximately 58-59% good outcomes and 14-15% mortality rates.

Reperfusion Success: Both groups demonstrated similarly high reperfusion rates, with "rates of successful reperfusion...similar between the groups (91% versus 95%)."

Safety Concern: "M2 patients had significantly increased rates of symptomatic ICH at 24 hours (4% versus 1%)," indicating a higher intracranial hemorrhage risk in the M2 group.

Clinical Implications: The authors concluded that while M2 thrombectomy produced comparable functional outcomes to M1 treatment, the increased hemorrhage risk warrants further investigation into whether mechanical thrombectomy benefits M2 occlusion patients.

--- [6] Variation in Vessel Size and Angiographic Outcomes Following Stent-Retriever Thr (Stroke: Vascular and Interventional Neurology, 2024) ---
DOI 10.1161/SVIN.123.000978 - STRATIS Vessel Size: Redirects to AHA Journals, access denied (403 error).

--- [7] Stent-Retriever Thrombectomy after Intravenous t-PA vs. t-PA Alone in Stroke (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
During a 2-year period at four centers in Catalonia, Spain, 206 patients who could be treated within 8 hours after the onset of symptoms of acute ischemic stroke were randomly assigned to receive either medical therapy (including intravenous alteplase when eligible) and endovascular therapy with the Solitaire stent retriever (thrombectomy group) or medical therapy alone (control group). All patients had confirmed proximal anterior circulation occlusion and the absence of a large infarct on neuroimaging. The primary outcome was the severity of global disability at 90 days. Among patients with anterior circulation stroke who could be treated within 8 hours after symptom onset, stent retriever thrombectomy reduced the severity of post-stroke disability and increased the rate of functional independence (43.7% versus 28.2% in controls). Symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage occurred at 1.9% in both groups.

--- [8] A Randomized Trial of Intraarterial Treatment for Acute Ischemic Stroke (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
HERMES Meta-Analysis - Background: The study pooled data from five 2015 randomized trials examining endovascular thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke caused by proximal anterior circulation artery occlusion. Methods: Researchers from the HERMES collaboration analyzed individual patient data from five trials (MR CLEAN, ESCAPE, REVASCAT, SWIFT PRIME, and EXTEND IA) conducted between December 2010 and December 2014. The primary outcome measured disability using the modified Rankin Scale at 90 days. Results: Endovascular thrombectomy produced significantly improved outcomes, with adjusted common odds ratio 2.49 (95% confidence interval 1.76-3.53; p<0.0001) compared to standard care. The number needed to treat was 2.6 patients to achieve one-level disability improvement for a single patient. Population Analyzed: 1,287 patients total (634 thrombectomy, 653 control). Subgroup Efficacy: Treatment benefits appeared consistent across diverse populations, with no heterogeneity of treatment effect across prespecified subgroups (p interaction=0.43). Notable effectiveness included elderly patients 80+ years and those treated beyond 300 minutes post-symptom onset. Safety: Mortality, parenchymal hematoma, and symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage rates did not differ significantly between groups. Conclusion: The analysis demonstrated endovascular thrombectomy benefits most stroke patients with large-vessel occlusion, regardless of demographics or geography.

--- [9] Randomized Assessment of Rapid Endovascular Treatment of Ischemic Stroke (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
SWIFT Trial - Background: The Solitaire Flow Restoration Device is a novel, self-expanding stent retriever designed to yield rapid flow restoration in acute cerebral ischaemia. Researchers compared this device against the Merci Retrieval System, the established mechanical thrombectomy standard. Methods: This randomized, parallel-group, non-inferiority trial enrolled 113 patients across 18 sites (17 USA, 1 France). Participants had acute ischemic stroke with moderate-to-severe deficits, treatable within 8 hours. The primary endpoint measured TIMI scale 2-3 blood flow without symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage after up to three device passes. Results: Primary Efficacy Outcome - Solitaire: 61% achieved target flow; Merci: 24% achieved target flow; Difference 37% [95% CI 19-53], odds ratio 4.87. 3-Month Neurological Outcomes - Solitaire: 58% showed good outcomes; Merci: 33% showed good outcomes; Difference 25% [6-43], odds ratio 2.78. Mortality at 90 Days - Solitaire: 17%; Merci: 38%; Difference -21% [-39 to -3], odds ratio 0.34. The trial demonstrated Solitaire's superior angiographic, safety, and clinical performance, leading to early termination via prespecified efficacy stopping rule.

--- [10] Endovascular Therapy for Ischemic Stroke with Perfusion-Imaging Selection (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
Background: The researchers investigated whether advanced imaging selection, newer devices, and early treatment could improve outcomes in endovascular stroke therapy, as prior trials showed variable results. Methods: Seventy patients with ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours of onset received either endovascular thrombectomy with a Solitaire FR stent retriever or alteplase alone. All participants had "occlusion of the internal carotid or middle cerebral artery" with salvageable brain tissue confirmed on CT perfusion imaging. Results: The trial stopped early due to efficacy. Endovascular therapy achieved superior reperfusion (median 100% vs. 37%, p<0.001) and improved early neurologic recovery at three days (80% versus 37%). Importantly, "more patients achieving functional independence (score of 0 to 2 on the modified Rankin scale, 71% vs. 40%; P=0.01)" at 90 days. No significant differences emerged in mortality or symptomatic bleeding rates. Conclusions: For patients with proximal arterial occlusion and salvageable tissue on imaging, early thrombectomy combined with initial alteplase "improved reperfusion, early neurologic recovery, and functional outcome" compared to alteplase monotherapy.

--- [11] Thrombectomy within 8 Hours after Symptom Onset in Ischemic Stroke (New England Journal of Medicine, 2015) ---
Background: The study tested whether advanced imaging selection, newer devices, and earlier intervention improve outcomes in endovascular stroke therapy. Methods: Researchers randomly assigned patients with ischemic stroke (within 4.5 hours of onset, receiving alteplase) to either endovascular thrombectomy with Solitaire FR stent retriever or alteplase alone. All participants had proximal arterial occlusion with salvageable tissue and ischemic core <70 ml on CT perfusion imaging. Key Outcomes: The trial stopped early after 70 patients due to efficacy. The endovascular group showed superior reperfusion at 24 hours (100% vs. 37%), greater early neurologic improvement at 3 days (80% vs. 37%), and better functional independence at 90 days (71% vs. 40%). There were no significant differences in rates of death or symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage. Conclusion: Early thrombectomy combined with alteplase, guided by CT perfusion imaging selection, significantly improved reperfusion, neurologic recovery, and functional outcomes compared to alteplase monotherapy in patients with proximal cerebral artery occlusion.

--- [12] Endovascular thrombectomy after large-vessel ischaemic stroke: a meta-analysis o (The Lancet, 2016) ---
The HERMES collaboration pooled individual patient data from five randomized trials (MR CLEAN, ESCAPE, REVASCAT, SWIFT PRIME, and EXTEND IA) conducted between December 2010 and December 2014. The analysis included 1,287 patients (634 assigned to endovascular thrombectomy, 653 assigned to control) with acute ischemic stroke caused by occlusion of the proximal anterior circulation artery. Thrombectomy significantly reduced disability compared to control (adjusted odds ratio 2.49, p<0.0001). The number needed to treat was 2.6. Benefits persisted across subgroups, including patients aged 80+, those treated >300 minutes after symptom onset, and those ineligible for alteplase. Mortality and hemorrhage rates showed no significant differences. Endovascular thrombectomy is of benefit to most patients with acute ischemic stroke caused by occlusion of the proximal anterior circulation.

--- [13] Safety and Efficacy of Solitaire Stent Thrombectomy: Individual Patient Data Met (Stroke, 2016) ---
The SEER meta-analysis combined individual patient data from four randomized trials (SWIFT PRIME, ESCAPE, EXTEND-IA, and REVASCAT) where Solitaire was the primary or only device used. The analysis involved 787 patients total: 401 receiving endovascular thrombectomy and 386 receiving standard care. The treatment demonstrated substantial benefit with a common odds ratio of 2.7 for improved outcomes. Approximately 40 of 100 treated patients experienced reduced disability, with 23 achieving independent functional status. The number needed to treat was 2.5 patients to improve disability by one level. Successful revascularization occurred in 77% of Solitaire-treated patients. No significant differences emerged in symptomatic hemorrhage or mortality rates between groups. Benefits remained consistent across all patient subgroups tested. Solitaire thrombectomy proved safe and highly effective with substantially reduced disability for large vessel ischemic stroke.

--- [14] North American SOLITAIRE Stent-Retriever Acute Stroke Registry (Stroke, 2013) ---
An investigator-initiated NASA registry recruited 24 clinical sites within North America to submit demographic, clinical, site-adjudicated angiographic, and clinical outcome data on consecutive patients treated with the Solitaire Flow Restoration device. The study examined 354 patients treated at 24 centers between March 2012 and February 2013. Recanalization success (TIMI ≥2) was achieved in 83.3% in NASA versus 83% in SWIFT. Modified Rankin Scale ≤2 at 90 days was observed in 42% of NASA patients versus 37% (SWIFT) and 40% (TREVO 2). 90-day mortality was 30.2% in NASA versus 17.2% (SWIFT) and 29% (TREVO 2). The investigators determined that the Solitaire FR device performance in clinical practice is comparable with the SWIFT and TREVO 2 trial results, demonstrating the device's effectiveness outside controlled trial settings.

--- [15] Solitaire flow restoration device versus the Merci Retriever in patients with ac (International Journal of Stroke, 2012) ---
This randomized non-inferiority trial enrolled 113 patients across 18 centers (primarily in the USA) with acute ischemic stroke requiring treatment within 8 hours. Patients received either Solitaire or Merci thrombectomy devices. The Solitaire group demonstrated superior performance: 61% achieved the primary efficacy endpoint versus 24% in the Merci group (odds ratio 4.87). Three-month neurological outcomes favored Solitaire (58% vs 33%), and mortality was lower with Solitaire (17% vs 38%). The Solitaire Flow Restoration Device achieved substantially better angiographic, safety, and clinical outcomes than did the Merci Retrieval System.

--- [16] Mechanical thrombectomy in acute stroke: prospective pilot trial of the solitair (American Journal of Neuroradiology, 2012) ---
This prospective, single-center trial evaluated mechanical thrombectomy feasibility, safety, and efficacy in patients with acute ischemic stroke using the Solitaire FR device under conscious sedation. Patients with large artery occlusions were treated within 6 hours (anterior circulation) or 24 hours (posterior circulation) of symptom onset. Among 36 patients treated between May 2010 and July 2011, successful revascularization occurred in 28 patients (77.8%). At 3 months, 22 patients (61.1%) achieved good functional outcomes (modified Rankin Scale ≤2). The procedure was technically feasible in 86.1% of cases, though the overall mortality rate reached 22.2%. Mechanical thrombectomy while under conscious sedation is feasible in a large percentage of cases and demonstrates favorable functional outcomes.

--- [17] Solitaire FR thrombectomy system: immediate results in 56 consecutive acute isch (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2018) ---
This study treated 56 consecutive acute stroke patients using intra-arterial therapy with the Solitaire FR system. The device successfully achieved recanalization in 50 of 56 patients (89%) with adequate TICI scores. Complications occurred in 5 patients, including two asymptomatic subarachnoid hemorrhages, two thromboembolic events, and one symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage. At discharge, 53.5% showed significant neurological improvement, and 46% achieved favorable functional outcomes. Solitaire FR is successful in achieving a high rate of arterial recanalization with a low complication rate, positioning it as an effective and safe thrombectomy option.

--- [18] Double solitaire mechanical thrombectomy in acute stroke: effective rescue strat (American Journal of Neuroradiology, 2014) ---
The Penumbra Pivotal Stroke Trial was a prospective, multicenter, single-arm study assessing safety and effectiveness of the Penumbra System for treating acute ischemic stroke caused by intracranial large vessel occlusive disease. The trial enrolled 125 patients with significant neurological deficits presenting within 8 hours of symptom onset. Participants had angiographically confirmed vessel occlusions and were either ineligible for or resistant to clot-dissolving medication. Successful revascularization occurred in approximately 82% of treated vessels. The procedure generated 18 adverse events in 16 patients (12.8%), with 3 serious events. Imaging revealed brain bleeding in 28% of patients within 24 hours, with symptomatic cases in 11.2%. Mortality reached 32.8% at 90 days, though 25% achieved favorable functional outcomes. The Penumbra System demonstrated safe and effective revascularization for stroke patients with large vessel blockages treated within the 8-hour window.

--- [19] Mechanical thrombectomy with the Solitaire AB device in acute ischemic stroke of (Neurology Journal, 2016) ---
The ASTER trial was a randomized, open-label, blinded end-point clinical trial conducted in 8 comprehensive stroke centers in France comparing contact aspiration versus stent retriever techniques for treating acute ischemic stroke with large vessel occlusion. Patients who presented with acute ischemic stroke and a large vessel occlusion in the anterior circulation within 6 hours of symptom onset were included. Patients were randomly assigned to first-line contact aspiration (n = 192) or first-line stent retriever (n = 189) immediately prior to mechanical thrombectomy. The primary outcome was the proportion of patients with successful revascularization defined as a modified Thrombolysis in Cerebral Infarction score of 2b or 3 at the end of all endovascular procedures. Among 381 randomized patients (mean age 69.9 years), 363 completed the trial. Successful revascularization occurred in 85.4% of the aspiration group versus 83.1% of the stent retriever group (P=.53). First-line thrombectomy with contact aspiration compared with stent retriever did not result in an increased successful revascularization rate at the end of the procedure, suggesting comparable effectiveness between the two techniques.

--- [20] Impact of age and baseline NIHSS scores on clinical outcomes in the mechanical t (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2014) ---
The COMPASS trial was a multicentre randomized trial evaluating whether direct aspiration as first pass treatment was non-inferior to stent retriever therapy for acute ischemic stroke from large-vessel occlusion. This trial enrolled 270 patients at 15 sites (10 US hospitals, 4 specialty clinics, 1 Canadian hospital). Participants had anterior circulation large-vessel occlusion within 6 hours of symptom onset. Patients were randomly assigned to either direct aspiration first pass or stent retriever first line thrombectomy. The primary outcome measured functional recovery at 90 days using the modified Rankin Scale (0-2 score), with a non-inferiority margin of 0.15. Aspiration achieved a modified Rankin score of 0-2 in 52% of patients versus 50% in the stent retriever group, demonstrating non-inferiority. Intracranial hemorrhage occurred in 36% of aspiration patients and 34% of stent retriever patients. Three-month mortality was 22% in both groups. The results support the use of direct aspiration as an alternative to stent retriever as first-line therapy for stroke thrombectomy.

--- [21] Intracranial thrombectomy using the Solitaire stent: a historical vignette (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2012) ---
A 67-year-old woman presented 3.5 hours after stroke onset with a right middle cerebral artery occlusion. Initial mechanical thrombectomy with a microbrush failed to achieve recanalization. The medical team then deployed a Solitaire stent within the blocked vessel. After several minutes of incubation, the expanded stent was slowly withdrawn under continuous aspiration with instantaneous removal of the entire thrombus and complete recanalization of the right MCA. The patient achieved a National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale score of 0 at 30-day follow-up, indicating complete recovery. Although initially designed for aneurysm treatment, the Solitaire stent proved safe and effective for thrombectomy, representing the first successful human clinical use of a Solitaire stent for this purpose and the ignition spark for the development of a whole generation of new devices, now called stent retrievers.

--- [22] Mechanical thrombectomy using a Solitaire stent retriever in the treatment of pe (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2019) ---
A prospective, single-center pilot study evaluated the safety and effectiveness of a self-expanding and fully retrievable stent (Solitaire AB) in revascularization of patients with acute ischemic stroke. The study included 20 patients with acute ischemic stroke attributable to a large artery occlusion of the anterior circulation within the first 8 hours from symptoms onset. Successful revascularization defined as thrombosis in cerebral ischemia grade 2b or 3 was achieved in 18 of 20 (90%) treated vessels, and 16 patients showed immediate restoration of flow after stent deployment. The average number of passes for maximal recanalization was 1.4, and the median time from groin puncture to recanalization was 50 (38-71) minutes. The Solitaire AB device can rapidly, safely, and effectively retrieve clots from major brain arteries within the treatment window.

--- [23] Solitaire stentectomy: 'deploy and engage' and 'loop and snare' techniques (Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery, 2016) ---
This article discusses mechanical thrombectomy using Solitaire FR stents for acute ischemic stroke caused by large vessel blockage. A significant complication is stent detachment during the procedure, which is associated with poor technical and clinical outcome. The paper presents two patient cases where conventional snare retrieval failed, prompting alternative techniques: the deploy and engage method and the loop and snare technique. Both approaches proved successful in removing the detached stents without requiring medications that could worsen acute stroke outcomes. The key advantage of retrieval techniques is the lack of need to administer fibrinolytic/antiplatelet agents that can potentially be catastrophic in the setting of acute stroke.

--- [24] Prospective, Multi-Centre, Single-Arm Study of Mechanical Thrombectomy using Sol (Stroke, 2013) ---
STAR was an international, multicenter, prospective, single-arm study of Solitaire FR thrombectomy in patients with large vessel anterior circulation strokes. A total of 202 patients were enrolled across 14 comprehensive stroke centers. Successful revascularization was achieved in 79.2% of patients. Favorable neurological outcomes occurred in 57.9% of participants, with device-related severe complications in 7.4% of cases. Mortality reached 6.9%, while intracranial bleeding occurred in 18.8% of patients, with only 1.5% being symptomatic. Solitaire FR treatment results in high rates of revascularization, low risk of clinically relevant procedural complications, and good clinical outcomes in combination with low mortality at 90 days.

--- [25] Technical Feasibility and Application of Mechanical Thrombectomy with the Solita (American Journal of Neuroradiology, 2013) ---
This study evaluated 14 consecutive patients with basilar artery occlusion (BAO) treated using the Solitaire FR Revascularization Device, often combined with thromboaspiration, thrombolysis, or additional stent placement. Overall, successful recanalization (TICI 3 or 2b) was achieved in all patients (TICI 3 in 78.6%, 11/14). Average device passes were 1.3, and median procedure time was 47 minutes. No device-related complications or symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage occurred. Good functional outcome (modified Rankin Scale 0-2) was achieved in 28.6% of patients. The multimodal endovascular approach demonstrated high recanalization rates with minimal complications, though underlying atherosclerotic lesions sometimes required permanent stent placement for complete vessel opening.

--- [26] Complications of Endovascular Treatment for Acute Stroke in the SWIFT Trial with (American Journal of Neuroradiology, 2013) ---
Twenty-three centers enrolled 634 consecutive patients treated between March 2013 and August 2015 using the Trevo device in the TRACK registry. Mean age was 66.1±14.8 years and mean baseline NIHSS score was 17.4±6.7; 86.7% had anterior circulation occlusions. The primary outcome was achieving a Thrombolysis in Cerebral Infarction (TICI) score of ≥2b. Among the 634 patients, 80.3% achieved successful revascularization. Forty-seven percent achieved favorable 90-day outcomes. Within guideline-recommended parameters (anterior circulation, 6-hour window), outcomes reached 51.4%. Mortality was 19.8%. The TRACK Registry validates that recent thrombectomy trial benefits translate to real-world practice, with treatment outcomes showing no significant differences between guideline-adherent and non-adherent cases.

--- [27] The INSPIRE Registry: Entering a New Era of Medical Device Research in the Neuro (Clinical Neuroradiology, 2020) ---
The Innovative Neurovascular Product Surveillance Registry (INSPIRE) is a unique, large scale, multitherapy, long-term observational study platform, sponsored by Medtronic Neurovascular, designed to continuously monitor the safety and performance of newly commercialized devices in real-world clinical settings. The first phase focuses on flow diverter devices (Pipeline Flex and Shield) with over 700 patients enrolled across 30 international sites. A second phase is enrolling stroke patients treated with Solitaire X and React 68/71 devices. Patients are prospectively enrolled from selected international sites whenever treated with the sponsor's new neurovascular devices, with all study data based on routine standard of care. The registry represents a collaborative approach between industry and the medical community, aligning with European medical device regulations regarding postmarketing surveillance.

--- [28] Solitaire Flow Restoration thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke: retrospective (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2013) ---
STORM-PE CAVT mechanical thrombectomy trial: This trial evaluated computer-assisted vacuum thrombectomy (CAVT) combined with anticoagulation versus anticoagulation alone for intermediate-high risk pulmonary embolism. The study enrolled 100 normotensive patients with acute PE (symptoms ≤14 days), elevated RV/LV ratios on imaging, and elevated cardiac biomarkers across 22 international sites. Results: The CAVT group demonstrated superior improvement in RV/LV ratio reduction (0.52±0.37) compared to anticoagulation alone (0.24±0.40), with a mean difference of 0.27 (95% CI, 0.12-0.43; P<0.001). Modified Miller scores showed greater improvements with CAVT versus anticoagulation alone at 48 hours (P<0.001). Early normalization of vital signs within 48 hours occurred more frequently following CAVT treatment. Major adverse event rates at 7 days were comparable between groups: CAVT 4.3% versus anticoagulation 7.5% (P=0.681). However, two PE-related deaths occurred in the CAVT arm.

data.Press Release Title: SEER Meta-Analysis Shows Solitaire Improves Functional Outcomes; Reduced Mortality in Patients 80+
data.Press Release Content: SEER Meta-Analysis: Solitaire Stent Retriever Functional Outcomes. The SEER (Safety and Efficacy of Solitaire Stent Thrombectomy) meta-analysis examined data from 787 patients across four randomized trials presented at the 2016 International Stroke Conference. Primary outcomes: Patients treated with Solitaire stent retriever plus IV-tPA demonstrated significantly better functional outcomes compared to IV-tPA alone with 54% vs 31.5%; P<.0001. The number needed to treat was remarkably low at 2.5, meaning approximately one patient per 2.5 treated showed improved functional results. For patients over 80 years old, the analysis revealed a substantial mortality reduction of 20% vs 40%; adjusted odds ratio, 3.7 (1.3–10.6); P=.01. Research indicated a time-dependent relationship, with outcomes declining as time from symptom onset to treatment increased. The device showed extremely low complication rates and low symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage. The meta-analysis synthesized results from SWIFT PRIME, REVASCAT, EXTEND-IA, and ESCAPE—all published in the New England Journal of Medicine in 2015.
