data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] The Penumbra Pivotal Stroke Trial: Safety and Effectiveness of a New Generation  (Stroke, 2009) ---
Penumbra SMART Coil System clinical evaluation: Background: The study aimed to evaluate long-term clinical outcomes for arteriovenous fistula (AVF) patients treated with the Penumbra SMART COIL System through a prospective, multicenter registry analysis. Methods: This subset analysis included AVF patients who underwent endovascular coiling using the SMART COIL system, with monitoring for 12 ± 6 months following treatment. Results: The analysis encompassed 41 patients. Safety: Zero procedural device-related serious adverse events occurred; periprocedural SAE rate was 2.4% (1/41), and no periprocedural deaths occurred. Technical Success: Complete occlusion was achieved in 87.8% (36/41) of patients, with 85.4% (35/41) of the patients avoiding catheter kickout complications. Follow-up Outcomes: At 1 year, the lesion occlusion was better or stable in 93.3% (28/30) of patients. Retreatment: Only 3.4% (1/29) of patients required retreatment during follow-up. Adverse Events: The SAE rate from 24 hours to one year was 26.8%, with 1-year all-cause mortality rate of 2.4% (1/41). Functional Status: At one-year follow-up, 90.9% (20/22) of patients had a modified Rankin Scale score within the range of 0 to 2.

--- [2] Effect of Endovascular Contact Aspiration vs Stent Retriever on Revascularizatio (JAMA, 2017) ---
SOFIA Plus catheter clinical outcomes SESAME/SOFAST registry: No PubMed article found matching this exact search.

--- [3] Aspiration thrombectomy versus stent retriever thrombectomy as first-line approa (The Lancet, 2019) ---
SOFIA catheter aspiration thrombectomy ASTER trial subanalysis: No PubMed article found matching this exact search.

--- [4] Endovascular Treatment of Acute Ischemic Stroke With the Penumbra System in Rout (Stroke, 2021) ---
SOFIA aspiration catheter real-world data: SOFIA 6F Plus Meta-Analysis Study: This research examined direct aspiration using SOFIA 6 F Plus catheters for large-vessel occlusion stroke treatment. The investigation included 144 consecutive patients plus a meta-analysis of nine articles totaling 758 patients. Results: Successful recanalization (mTICI 2b-3) rate of 75.7% was achieved with direct aspiration alone in the cohort. Functional independence at 90 days occurred in 40.3% of cases. Meta-analysis of pooled data revealed: mTICI 2b-3 rate was 71.6% (95%CI, 66.3-76.5%). Rescue stent-retriever backup was needed in 24.1% of cases. Overall success: mTICI 2b-3 rate after DA and rescue therapy was 88.9%. Functional independence: A pooled estimate of 45.6% achieved favorable outcomes. Mortality: 19% within 90 days. Hemorrhage risk: 5.8% (95%CI, 4.2-8.0%) of symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage.

--- [5] Aspiration thrombectomy with the Penumbra System for patients with stroke and la (Frontiers in Neurology, 2023) ---
ERIC retrieval device clinical evaluation: Background: The study evaluated safety and efficacy of mechanical thrombectomy using the ERIC device for acute ischemic stroke patients with distal medium vessel occlusions. Methods: A retrospective analysis examined 50 patients from a prospectively collected database, documenting clinical patient characteristics, procedural measures, timestamps at each stage, and patient outcomes. Results: The cohort included equal gender distribution (50% male/female) with median age 67 years and median NIHSS score of 14. First-pass recanalization outcomes showed: eTICI3 in 32%, eTICI-2c in 16%, eTICI-2B67 in 10%, eTICI2B50 in 6%, and mTICI 0-2A in 36%. The final recanalization rate achieved mTICI 2b-3 in 90% of cases and mTICI 2c-3 in 70%. Conclusion: The ERIC thrombectomy device offers a high rate of first-pass success along with a favorable safety profile.

--- [6] First-Pass Effect With Aspiration Thrombectomy With the Penumbra System in Patie (Stroke: Vascular and Interventional Neurology, 2022) ---
ERIC retrieval device multicenter study: No additional specific multicenter ERIC study found beyond Row 70.

--- [7] Penumbra, Inc. Announces Completion of THUNDER IDE Study Enrollment for Acute Is (Press Release, 2024) ---
Headway microcatheter clinical performance: The Headway27 microcatheter features optimal dimensions: 156 cm length, 2.6 Fr distal OD, ID 0.027 inches with excellent visibility for stroke procedures. Results: The study demonstrated strong clinical performance in 50 consecutive thrombectomy cases. Thrombectomy approaches: Manual aspiration alone occurred in 72% of cases, with stentriever-assisted techniques in 6% and combined methods in 22%. Recanalization success: mTICI 2B/3 recanalization was achieved in 94% of cases. Time efficiency: Median groin puncture to final recanalization time was 27 minutes. Vessel access: The device successfully reached all intended target vessels across M1, M2, M3, P2, P3, and basilar artery locations. Complication rate: Only 2 intra-procedural complications occurred, neither microcatheter-related. Device reliability: There were no cases requiring usage of an additional or alternative microcatheter.

--- [8] Randomized Controlled Trial of Mechanical Thrombectomy With Anticoagulation Vers (Circulation, 2025) ---
Background: Intermediate-high risk pulmonary embolism patients face elevated risk of clinical deterioration and mortality. The trial tested mechanical thrombectomy combined with anticoagulation against anticoagulation therapy alone. Methods: This international randomized controlled trial enrolled 100 patients across 22 sites with acute PE (symptoms ≤14 days), normotension, RV/LV ratio ≥1.0, and elevated cardiac biomarkers. Participants received either computer-assisted vacuum thrombectomy (CAVT) with anticoagulation (n=47) or anticoagulation alone (n=53). The primary endpoint measured RV/LV ratio changes at 48 hours via blinded imaging assessment. Results: CAVT demonstrated significantly greater RV/LV ratio reduction (0.52±0.37 versus 0.24±0.40; P<0.001). Pulmonary artery obstruction scores improved more substantially with CAVT. Vital sign normalization occurred more frequently within 48 hours following CAVT. Major adverse events within 7 days showed comparable rates between groups: "CAVT, 4.3% versus anticoagulation, 7.5%" (P=0.681). Two PE-related deaths occurred in the CAVT arm. Conclusions: CAVT proved superior to anticoagulation monotherapy for reducing RV/LV ratio within 48 hours, with faster vital sign normalization and comparable safety profiles regarding major adverse events.

--- [9] The SMART Registry: Long-Term Results on the Utility of the Penumbra SMART COIL  (Neurosurgical Focus, 2021) ---
EmboTrap II EXCELLENT registry results: Background: The mTICI 2B score indicates 50-99% reperfusion but represents a broad range. Researchers investigated whether very late leptomeningeal collaterals (VLLC) could further differentiate outcomes in this patient group. Method: A prospective registry of anterior circulation acute ischemic stroke patients treated with EmboTrap devices (2013-2016) was analyzed. Results: Among 177 anterior circulation stroke patients, 94 (53.1%) achieved only mTICI-2B reperfusion. Within this group, 16/94 (17.0%) patients achieved excellent functional outcomes at 3 months and 26 (27.7%) had hyperdensity on follow-up computed tomography. On univariate analysis, VLLC presence was inversely associated with excellent functional outcomes and positively associated with mortality in mTICI-2B patients. Multivariate analysis showed VLLC was inversely associated with excellent outcomes (odds ratio 0.075, 95% CI 0.007-0.765, P = 0.029) but not significantly associated with mortality.

--- [10] Effect of Thrombectomy With Combined Contact Aspiration and Stent Retriever vs S (JAMA, 2021) ---
Background: The study addresses uncertainty about whether combining contact aspiration with stent retriever improves outcomes compared to stent retriever alone for treating acute ischemic stroke caused by large vessel occlusion in the anterior circulation. Methods: This multicenter, randomized controlled trial enrolled 408 patients (405 analyzed) from 11 French stroke centers between October 2017 and May 2018. Participants had anterior circulation large vessel occlusion within 8 hours of symptom onset. Patients were randomly assigned to receive either combined contact aspiration plus stent retriever (205 patients) or stent retriever alone (203 patients). The primary endpoint measured successful reperfusion using the expanded Thrombolysis In Cerebral Infarction score. Results: The combined approach did not significantly improve the primary outcome. Successful near-total/total reperfusion (eTICI 2c/3) occurred in 64.5% of the combined group versus 57.9% of the stent retriever-only group (P = .17). However, the combined technique showed better results for intermediate reperfusion measures after the initial intervention alone (86.2% vs 72.3%, P < .001). Conclusions: Initial thrombectomy using combined contact aspiration and stent retriever did not significantly improve the rate of near-total or total reperfusion compared with stent retriever monotherapy, though the trial may have lacked sufficient power to detect smaller differences.

--- [11] Initial Data Presented for Penumbra's INSIGHT Registry and Subanalysis of the CO (Endovascular Today, 2022) ---
LVIS stent for intracranial aneurysm treatment: The objective was to evaluate the feasibility, efficacy, and safety of the Low-profile Visualized Intraluminal Support (LVIS) device for stent-assisted coiling of wide-necked intracranial aneurysms. Results: Nine studies encompassing 384 patients and 390 aneurysms were analyzed. Technical Success: 96.8% (95% CI 94.4% to 99.1%) achievement rate. Complete Occlusion: 54.6% (95% CI 31.8% to 77.4%) immediately after treatment, improving to 84.3% (95% CI 78.9% to 89.7%) at follow-up. Safety Profile: Procedure-related morbidity was 1.4% (95% CI 0.2% to 2.6%) with zero mortality. Complications: Thromboembolic events occurred in 4.9% (95% CI 1.9% to 7.9%) of cases; hemorrhagic complications were 2.1% (95% CI 0.7% to 3.5%).

--- [12] Technical note and first results on JET 7 thromboaspiration device for T-ICA occ (BMC Neurology, 2022) ---
LVIS EVO stent clinical data: The LVIS EVO is a braided, self-expanding stent approved in Europe (2019) and the US (2024) for treating intracranial aneurysms. Researchers conducted a meta-analysis examining safety outcomes, occlusion rates, and technical success using data from PubMed, MEDLINE, and Embase through August 2024. The study included seven studies evaluating 422 patients with 435 aneurysms. Results: Technical success rate was 99.3% (95% CI, 98.5%-100%). Occlusion Rates: Immediate post-procedure: 75.5% (95% CI, 62.1%-89.0%); At follow-up (mean 256 days): 86.8% (95% CI, 80.4%-93.2%). Complications: Overall rate: 7.4% (95% CI, 2.6%-12.2%); Treatment-related morbidity: 1.8% (95% CI, 0.5%-3.0%); Ischemic-thromboembolic events: 4.7% (95% CI, 1.5%-8.0%); Symptomatic events: 2.2% (95% CI, 0.6%-3.8%).

data.Press Release Title: Penumbra Pivotal Stroke Trial: 125 Patients, 81.6% Recanalization — Data Presented at ASA International Stroke Conference. Published in Stroke.
data.Press Release Content: The Penumbra Pivotal Stroke Trial: Safety and Effectiveness of a New Generation of Mechanical Devices for Clot Removal in Intracranial Large Vessel Occlusive Disease. This prospective, multicenter, single-arm study enrolled 125 patients with neurological deficits (National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale score ≥8), presented within 8 hours of symptom onset, and had an angiographic occlusion (TIMI Grade 0 or 1) of a treatable large intracranial vessel. Postprocedure, 81.6% of the treated vessels were successfully revascularized to TIMI 2 to 3. There were 18 procedural events reported in 16 patients (12.8%), with 3 patients (2.4%) having serious events. A total of 35 patients (28%) were found to have intracranial hemorrhage on 24-hour CT, of which 14 (11.2%) were symptomatic. All cause mortality was 32.8% at 90 days with 25% achieving modified Rankin Scale score of ≤2. The study was published in Stroke in August 2009. A notable finding was that while the device achieved excellent recanalization rates of 81.6%, the rates of independent functional outcome (mRS ≤2 at 90 days) were only 25%, lagging behind the high recanalization rates.

data.Press Release Title: ASTER RCT: No Significant Difference Between ADAPT (Penumbra) and Stent Retriever as Frontline Strategy in Acute Stroke
data.Press Release Content:
ASTER RCT: ADAPT vs Stent Retriever for Stroke Treatment

Key Finding
The ASTER randomized controlled trial found no statistically significant difference between ADAPT (direct aspiration first pass technique) and stent retriever as frontline strategies for treating large vessel occlusion strokes.

Study Details
- Presented by: Bertrand Lapergue at the International Stroke Conference (February 22-24, 2017, Houston)
- Sample: 381 patients across 8 French centers (189 assigned to stent retriever, 192 to ADAPT)
- Study period: October 2015 for 12 months

Primary Results
- Stent retriever group: 157 patients (83.1%) achieved successful reperfusion (mTICI 2b/3)
- ADAPT group: 164 patients (85.4%) achieved successful reperfusion
- P-value: 0.53 (not significant)

Notable Finding
Time from clot contact to reperfusion was slightly shorter with aspiration (13 minutes vs. 23 minutes), though overall procedural times showed minimal differences between approaches.

Key Quote
The ASTER trial showed no statistically significant difference between aspiration and stent retriever as a frontline thrombectomy approach with similar efficacy and safety endpoints.

Funding
Penumbra provided unrestricted research support but had no involvement in study design, conduct, or manuscript preparation.

data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] The Penumbra Pivotal Stroke Trial: Safety and Effectiveness of a New Generation  (Stroke, 2009) ---
Penumbra SMART Coil System clinical evaluation: Background: The study aimed to evaluate long-term clinical outcomes for arteriovenous fistula (AVF) patients treated with the Penumbra SMART COIL System through a prospective, multicenter registry analysis. Methods: This subset analysis included AVF patients who underwent endovascular coiling using the SMART COIL system, with monitoring for 12 ± 6 months following treatment. Results: The analysis encompassed 41 patients. Safety: Zero procedural device-related serious adverse events occurred; periprocedural SAE rate was 2.4% (1/41), and no periprocedural deaths occurred. Technical Success: Complete occlusion was achieved in 87.8% (36/41) of patients, with 85.4% (35/41) of the patients avoiding catheter kickout complications. Follow-up Outcomes: At 1 year, the lesion occlusion was better or stable in 93.3% (28/30) of patients. Retreatment: Only 3.4% (1/29) of patients required retreatment during follow-up. Adverse Events: The SAE rate from 24 hours to one year was 26.8%, with 1-year all-cause mortality rate of 2.4% (1/41). Functional Status: At one-year follow-up, 90.9% (20/22) of patients had a modified Rankin Scale score within the range of 0 to 2.

--- [2] Effect of Endovascular Contact Aspiration vs Stent Retriever on Revascularizatio (JAMA, 2017) ---
SOFIA Plus catheter clinical outcomes SESAME/SOFAST registry: No PubMed article found matching this exact search.

--- [3] Aspiration thrombectomy versus stent retriever thrombectomy as first-line approa (The Lancet, 2019) ---
SOFIA catheter aspiration thrombectomy ASTER trial subanalysis: No PubMed article found matching this exact search.

--- [4] Endovascular Treatment of Acute Ischemic Stroke With the Penumbra System in Rout (Stroke, 2021) ---
SOFIA aspiration catheter real-world data: SOFIA 6F Plus Meta-Analysis Study: This research examined direct aspiration using SOFIA 6 F Plus catheters for large-vessel occlusion stroke treatment. The investigation included 144 consecutive patients plus a meta-analysis of nine articles totaling 758 patients. Results: Successful recanalization (mTICI 2b-3) rate of 75.7% was achieved with direct aspiration alone in the cohort. Functional independence at 90 days occurred in 40.3% of cases. Meta-analysis of pooled data revealed: mTICI 2b-3 rate was 71.6% (95%CI, 66.3-76.5%). Rescue stent-retriever backup was needed in 24.1% of cases. Overall success: mTICI 2b-3 rate after DA and rescue therapy was 88.9%. Functional independence: A pooled estimate of 45.6% achieved favorable outcomes. Mortality: 19% within 90 days. Hemorrhage risk: 5.8% (95%CI, 4.2-8.0%) of symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage.

--- [5] Aspiration thrombectomy with the Penumbra System for patients with stroke and la (Frontiers in Neurology, 2023) ---
ERIC retrieval device clinical evaluation: Background: The study evaluated safety and efficacy of mechanical thrombectomy using the ERIC device for acute ischemic stroke patients with distal medium vessel occlusions. Methods: A retrospective analysis examined 50 patients from a prospectively collected database, documenting clinical patient characteristics, procedural measures, timestamps at each stage, and patient outcomes. Results: The cohort included equal gender distribution (50% male/female) with median age 67 years and median NIHSS score of 14. First-pass recanalization outcomes showed: eTICI3 in 32%, eTICI-2c in 16%, eTICI-2B67 in 10%, eTICI2B50 in 6%, and mTICI 0-2A in 36%. The final recanalization rate achieved mTICI 2b-3 in 90% of cases and mTICI 2c-3 in 70%. Conclusion: The ERIC thrombectomy device offers a high rate of first-pass success along with a favorable safety profile.

--- [6] First-Pass Effect With Aspiration Thrombectomy With the Penumbra System in Patie (Stroke: Vascular and Interventional Neurology, 2022) ---
ERIC retrieval device multicenter study: No additional specific multicenter ERIC study found beyond Row 70.

--- [7] Penumbra, Inc. Announces Completion of THUNDER IDE Study Enrollment for Acute Is (Press Release, 2024) ---
Headway microcatheter clinical performance: The Headway27 microcatheter features optimal dimensions: 156 cm length, 2.6 Fr distal OD, ID 0.027 inches with excellent visibility for stroke procedures. Results: The study demonstrated strong clinical performance in 50 consecutive thrombectomy cases. Thrombectomy approaches: Manual aspiration alone occurred in 72% of cases, with stentriever-assisted techniques in 6% and combined methods in 22%. Recanalization success: mTICI 2B/3 recanalization was achieved in 94% of cases. Time efficiency: Median groin puncture to final recanalization time was 27 minutes. Vessel access: The device successfully reached all intended target vessels across M1, M2, M3, P2, P3, and basilar artery locations. Complication rate: Only 2 intra-procedural complications occurred, neither microcatheter-related. Device reliability: There were no cases requiring usage of an additional or alternative microcatheter.

--- [8] Randomized Controlled Trial of Mechanical Thrombectomy With Anticoagulation Vers (Circulation, 2025) ---
Background: Intermediate-high risk pulmonary embolism patients face elevated risk of clinical deterioration and mortality. The trial tested mechanical thrombectomy combined with anticoagulation against anticoagulation therapy alone. Methods: This international randomized controlled trial enrolled 100 patients across 22 sites with acute PE (symptoms ≤14 days), normotension, RV/LV ratio ≥1.0, and elevated cardiac biomarkers. Participants received either computer-assisted vacuum thrombectomy (CAVT) with anticoagulation (n=47) or anticoagulation alone (n=53). The primary endpoint measured RV/LV ratio changes at 48 hours via blinded imaging assessment. Results: CAVT demonstrated significantly greater RV/LV ratio reduction (0.52±0.37 versus 0.24±0.40; P<0.001). Pulmonary artery obstruction scores improved more substantially with CAVT. Vital sign normalization occurred more frequently within 48 hours following CAVT. Major adverse events within 7 days showed comparable rates between groups: "CAVT, 4.3% versus anticoagulation, 7.5%" (P=0.681). Two PE-related deaths occurred in the CAVT arm. Conclusions: CAVT proved superior to anticoagulation monotherapy for reducing RV/LV ratio within 48 hours, with faster vital sign normalization and comparable safety profiles regarding major adverse events.

--- [9] The SMART Registry: Long-Term Results on the Utility of the Penumbra SMART COIL  (Neurosurgical Focus, 2021) ---
EmboTrap II EXCELLENT registry results: Background: The mTICI 2B score indicates 50-99% reperfusion but represents a broad range. Researchers investigated whether very late leptomeningeal collaterals (VLLC) could further differentiate outcomes in this patient group. Method: A prospective registry of anterior circulation acute ischemic stroke patients treated with EmboTrap devices (2013-2016) was analyzed. Results: Among 177 anterior circulation stroke patients, 94 (53.1%) achieved only mTICI-2B reperfusion. Within this group, 16/94 (17.0%) patients achieved excellent functional outcomes at 3 months and 26 (27.7%) had hyperdensity on follow-up computed tomography. On univariate analysis, VLLC presence was inversely associated with excellent functional outcomes and positively associated with mortality in mTICI-2B patients. Multivariate analysis showed VLLC was inversely associated with excellent outcomes (odds ratio 0.075, 95% CI 0.007-0.765, P = 0.029) but not significantly associated with mortality.

--- [10] Effect of Thrombectomy With Combined Contact Aspiration and Stent Retriever vs S (JAMA, 2021) ---
Background: The study addresses uncertainty about whether combining contact aspiration with stent retriever improves outcomes compared to stent retriever alone for treating acute ischemic stroke caused by large vessel occlusion in the anterior circulation. Methods: This multicenter, randomized controlled trial enrolled 408 patients (405 analyzed) from 11 French stroke centers between October 2017 and May 2018. Participants had anterior circulation large vessel occlusion within 8 hours of symptom onset. Patients were randomly assigned to receive either combined contact aspiration plus stent retriever (205 patients) or stent retriever alone (203 patients). The primary endpoint measured successful reperfusion using the expanded Thrombolysis In Cerebral Infarction score. Results: The combined approach did not significantly improve the primary outcome. Successful near-total/total reperfusion (eTICI 2c/3) occurred in 64.5% of the combined group versus 57.9% of the stent retriever-only group (P = .17). However, the combined technique showed better results for intermediate reperfusion measures after the initial intervention alone (86.2% vs 72.3%, P < .001). Conclusions: Initial thrombectomy using combined contact aspiration and stent retriever did not significantly improve the rate of near-total or total reperfusion compared with stent retriever monotherapy, though the trial may have lacked sufficient power to detect smaller differences.

--- [11] Initial Data Presented for Penumbra's INSIGHT Registry and Subanalysis of the CO (Endovascular Today, 2022) ---
LVIS stent for intracranial aneurysm treatment: The objective was to evaluate the feasibility, efficacy, and safety of the Low-profile Visualized Intraluminal Support (LVIS) device for stent-assisted coiling of wide-necked intracranial aneurysms. Results: Nine studies encompassing 384 patients and 390 aneurysms were analyzed. Technical Success: 96.8% (95% CI 94.4% to 99.1%) achievement rate. Complete Occlusion: 54.6% (95% CI 31.8% to 77.4%) immediately after treatment, improving to 84.3% (95% CI 78.9% to 89.7%) at follow-up. Safety Profile: Procedure-related morbidity was 1.4% (95% CI 0.2% to 2.6%) with zero mortality. Complications: Thromboembolic events occurred in 4.9% (95% CI 1.9% to 7.9%) of cases; hemorrhagic complications were 2.1% (95% CI 0.7% to 3.5%).

--- [12] Technical note and first results on JET 7 thromboaspiration device for T-ICA occ (BMC Neurology, 2022) ---
LVIS EVO stent clinical data: The LVIS EVO is a braided, self-expanding stent approved in Europe (2019) and the US (2024) for treating intracranial aneurysms. Researchers conducted a meta-analysis examining safety outcomes, occlusion rates, and technical success using data from PubMed, MEDLINE, and Embase through August 2024. The study included seven studies evaluating 422 patients with 435 aneurysms. Results: Technical success rate was 99.3% (95% CI, 98.5%-100%). Occlusion Rates: Immediate post-procedure: 75.5% (95% CI, 62.1%-89.0%); At follow-up (mean 256 days): 86.8% (95% CI, 80.4%-93.2%). Complications: Overall rate: 7.4% (95% CI, 2.6%-12.2%); Treatment-related morbidity: 1.8% (95% CI, 0.5%-3.0%); Ischemic-thromboembolic events: 4.7% (95% CI, 1.5%-8.0%); Symptomatic events: 2.2% (95% CI, 0.6%-3.8%).

data.Press Release Title: Penumbra Launches Access25 Delivery Microcatheter Designed for Brain Aneurysm Access
data.Press Release Content:
Penumbra Access25 Delivery Microcatheter Launch

Announcement Overview
Penumbra has launched the Access25, a single-lumen delivery microcatheter designed to facilitate physician access to blood vessels in the brain for coil-based aneurysm treatment.

Device Details
- Compatibility: Works with Penumbra's Midway intermediate catheters and Benchmark/BMX guide catheters
- Coil Platform: Designed for 0.020-inch coils (PC400, POD400, PAC400)
- Tip Configurations: Available in straight, 45-degree, 90-degree, and 130-degree angles
- Key Features: Soft precision technology, advanced flexibility, optimized midsection, and tactile support

Clinical Benefits
According to Penumbra, physicians using PC400 coils have reported:
- Lower aneurysm recurrence rates
- Higher coil packing density
- Fewer coils required per procedure
- Reduced procedure times (compared to 10-system coils)

Key Quotes
Scott McPherson (St Dominic's Hospital): The new Access25 delivery microcatheter has not only proven its tracking capabilities in numerous procedures, but it has also showcased stability amidst procedural complexities in aneurysm management.

Navraj Heran (Royal Columbian Hospital): The design of Access25 allows the distal tip to deliver the coil as if it were painting it into the aneurysms during deployment.

Regulatory Status
No FDA clearance or regulatory pathway information provided in announcement.

Publication Date
28 February 2025

data.Press Release Title: Boston Scientific Set to Re-Enter Neurovascular Market with Penumbra Acquisition
data.Press Release Content:
Boston Scientific to Acquire Penumbra for $14.5 Billion

Acquisition Details

Boston Scientific Corporation announced a definitive agreement to acquire Penumbra in a cash-and-stock transaction valued at $374 per share, representing approximately $14.5 billion in enterprise value. The deal is anticipated to close in 2026, likely in the first half of the year, pending stockholder approval and customary closing conditions.

Financial Impact

Boston Scientific plans to finance the roughly $11 billion cash portion through a combination of existing cash reserves and new debt. The acquisition is projected to be $0.06-$0.08 dilutive to adjusted earnings per share in the first full year post-close, neutral or slightly accretive in year two, and increasingly accretive thereafter.

Penumbra expects full-year 2025 reported revenue of approximately $1.4 billion, representing growth of 17.3-17.5% over the prior fiscal year.

Key Leadership Comments

Boston Scientific's CEO Mike Mahoney stated that the company is thrilled to combine the talents and shared values of both organizations. Penumbra's chairman and CEO Adam Elsesser will join Boston Scientific's board upon closing.

Strategic Rationale

The acquisition expands Boston Scientific's presence in fast-growing vascular segments, particularly in neurovascular solutions including access catheters, stroke revascularization devices, mechanical thrombectomy systems, and neuroembolization coils.

data.Press Release Title: COMPASS Study Finds ADAPT Non-Inferior to Stent Retriever for First-Line Mechanical Thrombectomy
data.Press Release Content:
COMPASS Study: ADAPT Non-Inferior to Stent Retriever for Stroke Treatment

Study Overview
The COMPASS trial evaluated ADAPT (aspiration) versus stent retriever as first-line mechanical thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke patients within six hours of symptom onset.

Key Findings
- Primary endpoint met: ADAPT demonstrated non-inferiority to stent retriever (p=0.0014)
  - 90-day modified Rankin Scale 0-2: 52% ADAPT vs. 49% stent retriever
- Secondary outcomes favored ADAPT:
  - TICI 2c or greater within 45 minutes: 50% ADAPT vs. 44% stent retriever
  - TICI 3 or greater within 45 minutes: 34% ADAPT vs. 23% stent retriever
  - Time to TICI 2b or greater: 22 minutes (ADAPT) vs. 33 minutes (stent retriever)

Trial Details
- 270 patients enrolled (134 aspiration, 136 stent retriever)
- 15 international centers
- Prospective, randomized, blinded design
- Funded by Penumbra (manufacturer)

Principal Investigator Comment
Lead researcher J. Mocco noted the results show more robust aspiration performance compared to the prior ASTER trial, potentially due to newer catheter technology (ACE68 vs. ACE64) and increased use of balloon guide catheters.

data.Press Release Title: Q'Apel's Walrus Balloon Guide Catheter Gains CE Mark Approval
data.Press Release Content: Q'Apel's Walrus Balloon Guide Catheter Gains CE Mark Approval. Announcement Date: March 28, 2023. Device: Walrus Balloon Guide Catheter (BGC). Regulatory: CE Mark approval under European Medical Devices Regulation. Application: Mechanical thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke. History: Utilized by neurointerventional physicians in US since 2019. Key Benefits: Provides BGC clinical benefits with ease of use; ability to navigate catheter into distal locations safely; associated with improved patient outcomes from stroke.
data.Related Articles:
[1] Not specified. "Walrus Balloon Guide Catheter for Stroke Intervention: Technical Considerations and Clinical Outcome". World Neurosurgery (2021). PMID: 34033957
[2] Not specified. "First experience with Walrus balloon guide catheter in a whole-body flow model". Interventional Neuroradiology (2023). PMID: 37640884
[3] Not specified. "Carotid artery revascularization using the Walrus balloon guide catheter: safety and feasibility fro". Interventional Neuroradiology (2021). PMID: 34686574
data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] Walrus Balloon Guide Catheter for Stroke Intervention: Technical Considerations  (World Neurosurgery, 2021) ---
This multicenter study evaluated the Ballast 088 radial access system in neuroendovascular procedures across 138 patients (mean age 66.7 years; 41.3% female). The researchers examined procedures including mechanical thrombectomy for stroke (31.4%), carotid stenting (24.6%), and aneurysm embolization (22%). The primary outcome measured whether the access catheter successfully reached its target vessel and completed the procedure without requiring a switch to femoral access. Results: The system achieved a 97.8% technical success rate with minimal complications. Specifically, the study documented one access site complication (temporary spasm; 0.7%) and no device-related complications. The procedure handled challenging anatomical variations including severe vessel tortuosity, different aortic arch types, and difficult subclavian artery angles. Conclusion: The authors determined that the Ballast radial access system proved both safe and feasible, successfully managing diverse interventional procedures while maintaining favorable outcomes through radial access, even in cases with complex vascular anatomy.

--- [2] First experience with Walrus balloon guide catheter in a whole-body flow model (Interventional Neuroradiology, 2023) ---
The pRESET for Occlusive Stroke Treatment (PROST) randomized clinical trial was a multicenter, prospective, randomized, controlled, open-label, adaptive, noninferiority trial comparing the pRESET and Solitaire stent retrievers for treatment of large vessel occlusion (LVO) stroke. Between October 2019 and February 2022, multicenter participation occurred across 19 research hospitals and/or universities in the US and 5 in Germany. Of 340 randomized patients, the procedure was completed in 322. The primary end point of 90-day functional independence was achieved by 95 patients (54.9%) in the pRESET group and in 96 (57.5%) in the Solitaire group. The pRESET retriever demonstrated noninferiority across clinical and angiographic endpoints. Symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage occurred in 0 pRESET patients versus 2 Solitaire patients. The pRESET stent retriever was noninferior to Solitaire for treating LVO stroke, offering a safe and effective alternative for flow restoration.

--- [3] Carotid artery revascularization using the Walrus balloon guide catheter: safety (Interventional Neuroradiology, 2021) ---
pRESET LITE clinical outcomes - Search returned 2 specific results for pRESET LITE clinical outcomes but full abstracts not available in search results page.

data.Press Release Content: Q'Apel Medical Launches New-Generation Hippo Aspiration System for Stroke Thrombectomy. Announcement Date: December 12, 2023. Device: 072 Hippo aspiration system paired with Cheetah guide companion for large vessel occlusion stroke. Key Innovation: Proprietary adaptive tip conforms to varying clot shapes/sizes; real-time visibility of clot engagement (previously unavailable); flexible Cheetah guide accelerates delivery. Regulatory: FDA clearance received. Clinical Perspectives: Raymond Turner (PRISMA Health) noted adaptive tip aids real-time decisions; Elad Levy highlighted speed advantages; Omar Tanweer emphasized compatibility and cost-benefits. CEO King Nelson stated enables complete stroke procedures when paired with Walrus balloon guide.

data.Press Release Content: Q'Apel Medical Announces FDA Clearance for Walrus Balloon Guide Catheter. Announcement Date: July 17, 2019. Device: Walrus Balloon Guide Catheter with 087 inner diameter. Specifications: Designed for neurovascular procedures and stroke treatment; enhanced trackability vs. previous BGC platforms. Clinical Significance: Addresses technical limitations hindering BGC adoption. Quote: Physician noted catheter compatibility critical to stroke treatment; professor expected widespread adoption. Timeline: Second FDA-cleared product from Q'Apel within 12 months; first was Wahoo Access Catheter with Selectflex technology.

data.Press Release Title: Q'Apel Medical Announces CE Mark Approval for Armadillo SelectFlex Neurovascular Access System
data.Press Release Content: Q'Apel Medical CE Mark Approval for Armadillo SelectFlex Neurovascular Access System. Announcement Date: January 13, 2025. Device: Armadillo SelectFlex 7F neurovascular access catheter system. Key Feature: Patented SelectFlex Technology allows operators to adjust catheter flexibility on-demand by injecting/withdrawing 0.2cc saline; low-profile enabling biaxial approach without tri-axial configurations. Purpose: Introduction of interventional devices into neurovasculature. Clinical Benefits: Dr. Pascal Jabbour (Thomas Jefferson University) noted one-and-done solution simplifying access procedures. Business Impact: CEO Jodie Fam stated allows hospital standardization, decreases inventory, reduces cost per procedure. Regulatory: CE mark received; international expansion planned post-US success.

data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] A retrospective review of safety and efficacy of the selectFlex neurovascular ac (Interventional Neuroradiology, 2025) ---
pRESET LITE for M2 occlusions - Multicenter evaluation examined pRESET LITE stent retriever's effectiveness for medium vessel occlusions, including M2 segments. 227 acute ischemic stroke patients analyzed across 37 academic institutions. RESULTS: For proximal MeVOs (including M2): Successful reperfusion of the target vessel (mTICI 2b/2c/3): 85% of proximal MeVO, Median Passes: 2 overall, Complications: 7% periprocedural complication rate, Clinical Outcomes: 58% of all medium vessel occlusion patients demonstrated favorable outcomes at 3 months (modified Rankin Scale 0-2), Hemorrhage: 39% of proximal cases had post-procedure hemorrhagic transformation, with minimal severe hemorrhage (1% ECASS-PH type). Study employed 'a combined approach in 96% of cases,' utilizing the pRESET LITE with an aspiration catheter.

data.Press Release Title: Q'Apel Medical Announces U.S. FDA Clearance for the Zebra Neurovascular Access System
data.Press Release Content: Q'Apel Medical Announces U.S. FDA Clearance for the Zebra Neurovascular Access System and Prepares for Full U.S. Launch. Q'Apel Medical announced FDA clearance for its Zebra Neurovascular Access System on July 8, 2025. The device comes in 6F and 7F sizes and is designed to introduce interventional devices into peripheral and neurovasculature. The Zebra system features laser-cut stripes along its length, engineered to balance flexibility with structural support, maximizing the inner lumen without ledges to provide optimal support during device delivery. The 7F model accommodates 0.072" aspiration catheters, while the 6F design expands compatibility options for complex neurovascular procedures. Jan-Karl Burkhardt, MD, Division Head of Cerebrovascular Surgery at Penn Medicine, praised the device's performance: 'I continue to be impressed by Zebra's dynamic flexibility with each additional case.' The announcement comes amid rising demand for neurovascular interventions driven by an aging population. Q'Apel showcased the system at the Society for Neurointerventional Surgery conference in Nashville, July 14-18, 2025.

feature_claims.text: The Tenzing Delivery Catheter family provides a range of sizes specifically designed for mechanical thrombectomy and large-bore device delivery in acute ischemic stroke interventions. The catheters feature soft tapered tips designed to follow the natural flow of blood and seek the primary vessel, reducing ledge effects and vessel wall trauma during advancement and device deployment.

The Tenzing 7 Delivery Catheter was evaluated in a multicenter clinical study for delivering large-bore reperfusion catheters to the clot face during mechanical thrombectomy in acute ischemic strokes. Study results demonstrated 95.9% successful aspiration catheter delivery without requiring a stent-retriever to anchor the device, establishing strong efficacy in rapid large-vessel revascularization. The soft tapered tip design reduces ledge effect, a critical factor in smooth catheter advancement
- "95.9% Delivery Success": Tenzing 7 demonstrated exceptional aspiration catheter delivery rates

The 088 Access System is a two-component platform consisting of the Tenzing 8 guide catheter and the 088 Neurovascular Catheter. Designed for intracranial access to support thrombectomy procedures, the system provides a large lumen for aspiration compatibility with a trackable distal design. The 088 Access System complements Route 92 Medical's FreeClimb 88 reperfusion system for comprehensive large-bore thrombectomy capability.
- Provides stable cervical platform
System Features:
- Two-component design enables stable cervical-to-intracranial access
- Trackable distal design enables navigation to intracranial targets
- Proven in acute ischemic stroke thrombectomy procedures
- Large lumen enables aggressive aspiration and retrieval techniques
- Proven platform for large-bore thrombectomy in AIS
=== KEY FEATURES ===

FreeClimb® System is an advanced access system designed to provide superior performance — even in the most tortuous anatomy. The system comprises multiple components working together for enhanced deliverability.
- Designed to eliminate the need for microcatheter and guidewire in many cases
- Tapered, atraumatic tip designed to follow the natural flow of blood and seek the primary vessel
- Provides access and a stable base for delivering large-bore and super-bore catheters
- Enables clinicians to reach the target site without guidewire in 71% of cases

data.Press Release Title: Route 92 Medical's FreeClimb 54 Reperfusion System Cleared by FDA
data.Press Release Content: Route 92 Medical's FreeClimb 54 Reperfusion System Cleared by FDA. Announcement Date: April 30, 2024. Regulatory: 510(k) FDA clearance received. Device: FreeClimb 54 aspiration catheter + Tenzing 5 delivery catheter system. Intended Use: Endovascular thrombectomy to reach/extract stroke-causing clots; telescopes through complementary products; supports multiple passes and compatibility with other technologies; addresses distal clots when first-line therapies insufficient. Clinical Advantages: Dr. Peter Pema (Riverside Methodist Hospital) noted combines Tenzing delivery system effectiveness with aspiration catheter reaching far into cerebral circulation. Dr. Ronald Budzik (Ohio Health) called it game changer - simple system with larger-bore aspiration catheter reaching distal anatomy around tight turns.

data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] Improved catheter delivery for aspiration thrombectomy using Tenzing 7 ledge red (Interventional Neuroradiology, 2023) ---
Esperance aspiration catheter initial experience (Wallaby/phenox) - No results found in PubMed database.

--- [2] First-in-Human Experience: Route 92 Medical 132 cm FreeClimb 88 Catheter for Ant (Stroke: Vascular and Interventional Neurology, 2023) ---
Background: The research addresses contact aspiration mechanical thrombectomy (CAMT) using 0.088-inch catheters, which may improve first-pass success rates but face delivery challenges. Methods: Researchers conducted a retrospective analysis of 53 consecutive patients across eight sites who received off-label CAMT using the FreeClimb 88 and Tenzing 8 devices for large vessel occlusions in the internal carotid artery (ICA) or M1 segment. Results: Key findings included: Successful catheter delivery in 50 of 53 patients (94.3%). First-pass TICI 2c or 3 was achieved in 36 (67.9%) cases. Nine additional cases (18%) required smaller devices for distal occlusions after initial recanalization. Zero procedural complications or symptomatic hemorrhages occurred. Conclusion: The authors concluded that this approach proved safe, effective, and efficient with strong first-pass recanalization rates and no adverse events in their early experience with these devices.

--- [3] SUMMIT MAX: A Randomized Trial of the Super Large Bore HiPoint Reperfusion Syste (Stroke, 2025) ---
BACKGROUND: The SUMMIT MAX trial was a randomized controlled study comparing the super large bore HiPoint Reperfusion system against the Vecta Aspiration system for treating large vessel occlusion stroke.

METHODS: Patients with internal carotid artery and middle cerebral artery M1 occlusions were randomized to treatment with either the HiPoint 88 or Vecta aspiration systems. The study enrolled 250 patients, with 166 meeting criteria for the modified intent-to-treat analysis (89 HiPoint, 77 Vecta). Median patient age was 69 years with 54.2% female participants.

RESULTS: Successful reperfusion (modified Treatment in Cerebral Infarction ≥2b) was achieved in 77.5% (HiPoint) vs. 50.6% (Vecta), p<0.0001. First pass effect mTICI≥2c was 68% for HiPoint with 96% successful M1 delivery. Symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage within 24 hours was 3.6% (HiPoint) vs. 2.7% (Vecta), with 0% sICH in the HiPoint arm. At 90 days, good functional outcome (modified Rankin Scale ≤2) occurred in 50.6% of the HiPoint group and 53.3% of the Vecta group.

CONCLUSIONS: The super large bore HiPoint system was noninferior to the Vecta system in achieving successful reperfusion, with a similar safety profile, supporting its use for mechanical thrombectomy in anterior large vessel occlusion.

--- [4] A prospective, randomized, controlled, interventional clinical trial to evaluate (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2024) ---
The ASTER-2 trial was a multicenter, randomized trial that enrolled 408 patients across 11 French stroke centers from October 2017 to May 2018, with 12-month follow-up. Patients with anterior circulation large vessel occlusion within 8 hours of symptom onset participated. Participants received either combined contact aspiration plus stent retriever (205 patients) or stent retriever alone (203 patients). The study measured expanded Thrombolysis In Cerebral Infarction score of 2c or 3 (eTICI 2c/3), indicating near-total to total reperfusion. Among 405 analyzed patients, the combined approach showed 64.5% eTICI 2c/3 versus 57.9% for stent retriever alone—not statistically significant. However, the combined technique achieved better intermediate reperfusion rates (86.2% versus 72.3%). Combined contact aspiration and stent retriever did not significantly improve final reperfusion compared to stent retriever monotherapy, though the study may have lacked sufficient power for detecting smaller differences.

--- [5] Tenzing Assisted Delivery of Aspiration (TADA) technique for thrombectomy of med (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2024) ---
Neuroform Atlas HDE cohort - Prospective, multicenter clinical trial evaluated Neuroform Atlas Stent System for treating unruptured wide-necked intracranial aneurysms. RESULTS: Patient Population: 30 patients enrolled across 8 U.S. centers, 27 completed 12-month angiographic follow-up, Mean age 59.4 years; 80% female, Mean aneurysm size 5.3 mm. Primary Efficacy Endpoint: 'The primary efficacy endpoint was observed in 26/30 patients (86.7%, 95% CI 69.3% to 96.2%)' achieving complete aneurysm occlusion without retreatment or significant vessel narrowing. Safety Outcomes: Procedural success rate: 100% for stent deployment, 'The primary safety endpoint of stroke occurred in one patient (3.3%), who made a complete clinical recovery' at discharge, Zero neurological deaths reported. Conclusion: 'the Neuroform Atlas stent in conjunction with coils' delivered high angiographic occlusion rates with improved safety profile.

data.Press Release Title: Route 92 Medical Announces Results from Independent Real-World Study and Plans for 500-Patient SUMMIT RISE Study
data.Press Release Content: Real World Data Supports Results of Route 92 Medical's SUMMIT MAX Clinical Trial. Route 92 Medical announced results from an independent real-world study of their HiPoint Reperfusion System, presented at the Society of NeuroInterventional Surgery annual meeting in July 2025. The retrospective multicenter study analyzed 193 consecutive patients treated with the HiPoint system who were not enrolled in the SUMMIT MAX trial. Clinicians successfully delivered the HiPoint Reperfusion System to the stroke-causing occlusion in 96.2% of cases. First-Pass Effect (mTICI 2c/3) was achieved in 57.5% of cases, and First-Pass Effect (mTICI 2b/2c/3) was achieved in 68.4% of cases. The median number of passes required was 1. Dr. Daniel Tonetti noted the results 'demonstrate strong delivery and first pass effect data, as well as low complication rates,' consistent with findings from the SUMMIT MAX trial. Route 92 Medical also introduced SUMMIT RISE, a prospective 500-patient study designed to evaluate their neurovascular interventional solutions across diverse clinical cases with independent core-lab adjudication.

data.Press Release Title: Route 92 Medical Announces Results of SUMMIT MAX Clinical Trial — Highest First Pass Effect Ever Reported in Randomized Stroke Thrombectomy Study
data.Press Release Content: Route 92 Medical Announces Results of SUMMIT MAX Clinical Trial Showing Highest First Pass Effect Ever Reported in a Randomized Endovascular Stroke Thrombectomy Study. Route 92 Medical announced positive results from its SUMMIT MAX randomized controlled trial (NCT05018650) on May 21, 2025. The trial compared the efficacy and safety of the HiPoint® Reperfusion System including the super-bore .088" HiPoint 88 Aspiration Catheter to a conventional .074" catheter. The trial involved 250 patients across 31 centers. The HiPoint 88 Aspiration Catheter was successfully delivered to stroke occlusions in 89% of cases. At the three highest-enrolling sites, results were stronger with 96% delivery rates and 84% first-pass effect achievement. First-pass effect was significantly higher in the Route 92 Medical arm versus the conventional arm for FPE mTICI ≥2b (84% versus 53%; p=0.02) and FPE mTICI ≥2c (68% versus 30%; p=0.007). There were no cases of sICH in the Route 92 Medical arm. Patients in the Route 92 Medical arm required significantly less adjunctive devices (4% versus 53%; p < 0.0001). The company received 510(k) NRY clearance for its HiPoint Reperfusion System, making Route 92 Medical the first neurovascular intervention company to receive clearance for direct aspiration of a stroke-causing thrombus with a super-bore .088" catheter.

data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] Improved catheter delivery for aspiration thrombectomy using Tenzing 7 ledge red (Interventional Neuroradiology, 2023) ---
Esperance aspiration catheter initial experience (Wallaby/phenox) - No results found in PubMed database.

--- [2] First-in-Human Experience: Route 92 Medical 132 cm FreeClimb 88 Catheter for Ant (Stroke: Vascular and Interventional Neurology, 2023) ---
Background: The research addresses contact aspiration mechanical thrombectomy (CAMT) using 0.088-inch catheters, which may improve first-pass success rates but face delivery challenges. Methods: Researchers conducted a retrospective analysis of 53 consecutive patients across eight sites who received off-label CAMT using the FreeClimb 88 and Tenzing 8 devices for large vessel occlusions in the internal carotid artery (ICA) or M1 segment. Results: Key findings included: Successful catheter delivery in 50 of 53 patients (94.3%). First-pass TICI 2c or 3 was achieved in 36 (67.9%) cases. Nine additional cases (18%) required smaller devices for distal occlusions after initial recanalization. Zero procedural complications or symptomatic hemorrhages occurred. Conclusion: The authors concluded that this approach proved safe, effective, and efficient with strong first-pass recanalization rates and no adverse events in their early experience with these devices.

--- [3] SUMMIT MAX: A Randomized Trial of the Super Large Bore HiPoint Reperfusion Syste (Stroke, 2025) ---
BACKGROUND: The SUMMIT MAX trial was a randomized controlled study comparing the super large bore HiPoint Reperfusion system against the Vecta Aspiration system for treating large vessel occlusion stroke.

METHODS: Patients with internal carotid artery and middle cerebral artery M1 occlusions were randomized to treatment with either the HiPoint 88 or Vecta aspiration systems. The study enrolled 250 patients, with 166 meeting criteria for the modified intent-to-treat analysis (89 HiPoint, 77 Vecta). Median patient age was 69 years with 54.2% female participants.

RESULTS: Successful reperfusion (modified Treatment in Cerebral Infarction ≥2b) was achieved in 77.5% (HiPoint) vs. 50.6% (Vecta), p<0.0001. First pass effect mTICI≥2c was 68% for HiPoint with 96% successful M1 delivery. Symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage within 24 hours was 3.6% (HiPoint) vs. 2.7% (Vecta), with 0% sICH in the HiPoint arm. At 90 days, good functional outcome (modified Rankin Scale ≤2) occurred in 50.6% of the HiPoint group and 53.3% of the Vecta group.

CONCLUSIONS: The super large bore HiPoint system was noninferior to the Vecta system in achieving successful reperfusion, with a similar safety profile, supporting its use for mechanical thrombectomy in anterior large vessel occlusion.

--- [4] A prospective, randomized, controlled, interventional clinical trial to evaluate (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2024) ---
The ASTER-2 trial was a multicenter, randomized trial that enrolled 408 patients across 11 French stroke centers from October 2017 to May 2018, with 12-month follow-up. Patients with anterior circulation large vessel occlusion within 8 hours of symptom onset participated. Participants received either combined contact aspiration plus stent retriever (205 patients) or stent retriever alone (203 patients). The study measured expanded Thrombolysis In Cerebral Infarction score of 2c or 3 (eTICI 2c/3), indicating near-total to total reperfusion. Among 405 analyzed patients, the combined approach showed 64.5% eTICI 2c/3 versus 57.9% for stent retriever alone—not statistically significant. However, the combined technique achieved better intermediate reperfusion rates (86.2% versus 72.3%). Combined contact aspiration and stent retriever did not significantly improve final reperfusion compared to stent retriever monotherapy, though the study may have lacked sufficient power for detecting smaller differences.

--- [5] Tenzing Assisted Delivery of Aspiration (TADA) technique for thrombectomy of med (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2024) ---
Neuroform Atlas HDE cohort - Prospective, multicenter clinical trial evaluated Neuroform Atlas Stent System for treating unruptured wide-necked intracranial aneurysms. RESULTS: Patient Population: 30 patients enrolled across 8 U.S. centers, 27 completed 12-month angiographic follow-up, Mean age 59.4 years; 80% female, Mean aneurysm size 5.3 mm. Primary Efficacy Endpoint: 'The primary efficacy endpoint was observed in 26/30 patients (86.7%, 95% CI 69.3% to 96.2%)' achieving complete aneurysm occlusion without retreatment or significant vessel narrowing. Safety Outcomes: Procedural success rate: 100% for stent deployment, 'The primary safety endpoint of stroke occurred in one patient (3.3%), who made a complete clinical recovery' at discharge, Zero neurological deaths reported. Conclusion: 'the Neuroform Atlas stent in conjunction with coils' delivered high angiographic occlusion rates with improved safety profile.

data.Press Release Title: WOVEN Trial: 1-Year Follow-Up Shows 8.5% Stroke/Death Rate in Wingspan Stented Patients
data.Press Release Content: WOVEN trial assessed one-year follow-up outcomes for patients treated with Wingspan stent for symptomatic intracranial atherosclerotic disease. Device: Wingspan stent by Stryker Neurovascular. Population: 129 patients from original WEAVE trial (152 initially enrolled across 24 sites). Key Findings: 2.6% periprocedural stroke/death rate; at one-year: seven additional strokes occurred (six minor, one major), zero deaths in delayed follow-up, combined outcome of 11 strokes/deaths among 129 patients (8.5% at one year). Conclusion: Relatively low 8.5% 1-year stroke/death rate in stented patients treated according to on-label protocols. Published April 2021 in Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery.

data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] Stenting versus Aggressive Medical Therapy for Intracranial Arterial Stenosis (New England Journal of Medicine, 2011) ---
DOI 10.1161/STROKEAHA.119.028418 - Neuroform Atlas Stent System (ATLAS pivotal): Redirects to AHA Journals, access denied (403 error).

--- [2] Aggressive medical treatment with or without stenting in high-risk patients with (The Lancet, 2014) ---
PMID 32568654 - ATLAS 1-Year Outcomes (Neuroform Atlas Stent for Anterior Circulation Aneurysms)

ABSTRACT:
This pivotal IDE trial evaluated the safety and effectiveness of the Neuroform Atlas Stent System for treating wide-neck intracranial aneurysms in the anterior circulation. The study enrolled 182 patients across 25 U.S. centers with aneurysms having a neck width ≥4 mm or dome-to-neck ratio <2.

Patient Demographics:
- Mean age: 60.3 years; 73.1% female; 80.8% white
- Mean aneurysm size: 6.1 mm
- Mean neck width: 4.1 mm
- Most common locations: anterior communicating artery (35.2%), internal carotid artery ophthalmic segment (15.9%), middle cerebral artery bifurcation (14.8%)

RESULTS:
Primary Efficacy Endpoint: The study achieved complete aneurysm occlusion (Raymond-Roy class 1) without parent artery stenosis or retreatment in "84.7% (95% CI, 78.6%-90.9%)" of patients at 12-month follow-up.

Primary Safety Endpoint: "4.4% (8/182, 95% CI, 1.9%-8.5%)" of patients experienced major ipsilateral stroke or neurological death, meeting the <5% safety threshold.

Technical Success: 100% of stents were successfully placed in their intended anatomic locations.

Conclusion: The Neuroform Atlas stent combined with coiling demonstrated high rates of complete aneurysm occlusion at one year with excellent safety and technical success profiles.

--- [3] WEAVE Trial: Final Results in 152 On-Label Patients (Stroke, 2019) ---
PMID 32561658 - WEAVE Trial (Final Results)

ABSTRACT:
The WOVEN study assessed one-year follow-up outcomes from 129 patients treated with Wingspan stents for symptomatic intracranial atherosclerotic disease according to on-label indications. The original WEAVE trial documented "a 2.6% periprocedural stroke and death rate" across 152 patients at 24 sites.

RESULTS:
The one-year follow-up revealed seven delayed strokes in the cohort—six minor and one major. Importantly, "no deaths were recorded in the cohort" during the delayed period beyond the periprocedural phase.

Combined with four periprocedural events from the initial WEAVE study, the cumulative outcome was "11 strokes or deaths of the 129 patients (8.5%) at the 1-year follow-up."

Conclusion: The investigators concluded this demonstrated "a relatively low 8.5% 1-year stroke and death rate in stented patients," noting the cohort provided a more homogeneous patient population than prior studies examining Wingspan stent efficacy.

--- [4] The WOVEN trial: Wingspan One-year Vascular Events and Neurologic Outcomes (Neurosurgery, 2020) ---
PMID 21899409 - SAMMPRIS Trial (NEJM 2011, Wingspan)

ABSTRACT:
Background: Atherosclerotic intracranial arterial stenosis represents a significant stroke cause increasingly treated with percutaneous transluminal angioplasty and stenting (PTAS), though this approach had not been compared to medical management in randomized trials.

Methods: Researchers randomly assigned patients experiencing recent transient ischemic attack or stroke from 70-99% diameter stenosis of major intracranial arteries to either aggressive medical management alone or combined with PTAS using the Wingspan stent system. The primary endpoint measured "stroke or death within 30 days after enrollment or after a revascularization procedure" plus stroke in the qualifying artery territory beyond 30 days.

RESULTS:
The trial was terminated early after 451 patient randomizations due to unfavorable outcomes in the PTAS group:
- 30-day rates: PTAS showed "14.7% stroke or death" (12.5% nonfatal, 2.2% fatal) compared to "5.8% in the medical-management group"
- Extended follow-up: Beyond 30 days, stroke occurrence was similar between groups (13 patients each)
- One-year outcomes: PTAS group experienced "20.0%" primary endpoint events versus "12.2% in the medical-management group"

Conclusion: Aggressive medical therapy proved superior to PTAS with the Wingspan stent, primarily due to heightened early stroke risk from the procedural intervention.

data.Press Release Title: FDA Reminds Health Care Professionals About Risks of Wingspan When Used Outside FDA-Approved Indications
data.Press Release Content: FDA Warns Against Off-Label Use of Wingspan Stent System. The FDA issued a safety alert on April 25, 2019, regarding the Stryker Wingspan stent system used for intracranial arterial stenosis. The Wingspan Stent System is approved to open narrowed arteries in the brain of patients experiencing repeated strokes due to intracranial stenosis. Data from the mandated WEAVE postmarketing surveillance study revealed significantly higher complication rates with off-label use: stroke or death within 72 hours was 23.9% for off-label use versus 2.6% for approved indications, and stroke without death was 19.6% for off-label patients versus 1.3% for approved uses. All nine strokes in off-label patients occurred in the treated artery territory. Wingspan is approved only for patients meeting all criteria: age 22-80, at least two prior strokes despite aggressive medical therapy, most recent stroke more than 7 days prior, 70-99% intracranial artery stenosis from atherosclerosis, and modified Rankin Scale score of 3 or less. The FDA emphasized that physicians must receive Institutional Review Board approval to use Wingspan at clinical sites, reinforcing strict oversight of this Humanitarian Device Exemption product.

data.Press Release Title: Siemens Healthineers and Stryker Announce Partnership to Advance Robotic Neurovascular Interventions
data.Press Release Content:
# Siemens Healthineers and Stryker Partnership Announcement

## What It's About
Siemens Healthineers and Stryker announced a strategic partnership to develop an integrated robotic system for neurovascular procedures, including stroke and aneurysm treatment.

## Key Features
- First-of-its-kind collaboration combining neurovascular robotics, imaging guidance, and therapeutic devices
- Designed to manage multiple systems within a single ecosystem
- Focuses on improving procedural precision and reducing treatment time
- Encompasses robotic system design, access devices, implant integration, and imaging navigation

## Clinical Focus
The system aims to address:
- Emergency ischemic strokes (time-sensitive cases)
- Hemorrhagic strokes and complex aneurysms
- Current physician challenges managing multiple devices simultaneously

## Regulatory & Timeline Information
No FDA approval status or specific regulatory milestones mentioned. Partnership announcement date: September 17, 2025.

## Key Quotes

**Carsten Bertram** (Siemens Healthineers): "Stroke is the second-leading cause of death and the third-leading cause of disability in adults globally."

**Jim Marucci** (Stryker): "By combining our deep expertise...we aim to simplify workflows, enhance precision, and accelerate care."

## Development Phase
Initial partnership phase focuses on co-developing the integrated ecosystem and validating clinical value with leading physicians.

data.Press Release Title: Wingspan System Receives FDA Humanitarian Device Exemption (HDE) Approval for Intracranial Atherosclerotic Disease
data.Press Release Content:
Wingspan Stent System - FDA Humanitarian Device Exemption (HDE) H050001 - Original 2005 Approval

HDE Approval Details:
- HDE Number: H050001
- Approval Date: August 9, 2005
- Manufacturer: Boston Scientific (Fremont, CA)
- Regulatory Pathway: Humanitarian Device Exemption (FDA)

Device Description:
- Classification: Self-expanding nitinol intracranial stent
- Design: Tubular mesh construction with zigzag struts
- Material: Nitinol (nickel-titanium alloy)
- Deployment: Self-expanding mechanism upon release from catheter
- Target Location: Intracranial arteries

Indications for Use:
- Treatment of atherosclerotic lesions or accumulated plaque in brain arteries
- Symptomatic intracranial arterial stenosis
- Patients with ≥50% intracranial stenosis
- Patients with recurrent ischemic events despite antithrombotic therapy
- Intended for high-risk patient populations where alternatives are unavailable

Clinical Context at Time of Approval (2005):
- Previous experience: Mostly with balloon-mounted coronary stents
- Technical limitations: Coronary stents difficult to deliver in tortuous intracranial circulation
- Wingspan represented specialized design addressing intracranial vascular anatomy
- Need: No approved intracranial stent system existing at time

Humanitarian Device Exemption Status:
- HDE designation: Intended for conditions affecting <4,000 people per year in US
- Regulatory advantage: Allowed approval without Phase III clinical trials
- Marketing approval: Based on humanitarian use criteria rather than efficacy data
- Patient population: Small, high-risk cohort with limited treatment alternatives

System Components:
- Primary Component: Wingspan self-expanding stent
- Delivery System: Gateway PTA (percutaneous transluminal angioplasty) balloon catheter
- Combined System: Gateway-Wingspan combination for lesion preparation and stent placement

Technical Significance:
- First self-expanding intracranial stent specifically designed for brain vasculature
- Addressed delivery challenges in tortuous intracranial circulation
- Provided alternative to off-label coronary stent use
- Represented significant advancement in intracranial interventional technology

Clinical Application:
- Prevention of recurrent ischemic stroke in high-risk patients
- Treatment of symptomatic intracranial atherosclerotic disease
- Patient population: Those failing medical therapy with severe stenosis

Post-Approval Trajectory:
- Device initially well-received upon HDE approval in 2005
- Later studies and registry data provided additional evidence
- FDA subsequently revised indications based on post-market data
- SAMMPRIS trial (published 2011) compared stenting to medical therapy
- FDA issued safety communications regarding appropriate use

Publication/Announcement:
- Boston Scientific press release: August 9, 2005
- FDA HDE database: H050001
- Type of approval: Humanitarian Device Exemption (not traditional premarket approval)

Regulatory Significance:
This HDE approval represented a major regulatory milestone allowing expanded treatment options for intracranial atherosclerotic disease, even though it lacked the extensive clinical trial data required for standard FDA premarket approval.

--- [3] WEAVE Trial: Final Results in 152 On-Label Patients (Stroke, 2019) ---
PMID 32561658 - WEAVE Trial (Final Results)

ABSTRACT:
The WOVEN study assessed one-year follow-up outcomes from 129 patients treated with Wingspan stents for symptomatic intracranial atherosclerotic disease according to on-label indications. The original WEAVE trial documented "a 2.6% periprocedural stroke and death rate" across 152 patients at 24 sites.

RESULTS:
The one-year follow-up revealed seven delayed strokes in the cohort—six minor and one major. Importantly, "no deaths were recorded in the cohort" during the delayed period beyond the periprocedural phase.

Combined with four periprocedural events from the initial WEAVE study, the cumulative outcome was "11 strokes or deaths of the 129 patients (8.5%) at the 1-year follow-up."

Conclusion: The investigators concluded this demonstrated "a relatively low 8.5% 1-year stroke and death rate in stented patients," noting the cohort provided a more homogeneous patient population than prior studies examining Wingspan stent efficacy.

--- [4] The WOVEN trial: Wingspan One-year Vascular Events and Neurologic Outcomes (Neurosurgery, 2020) ---
PMID 21899409 - SAMMPRIS Trial (NEJM 2011, Wingspan)

ABSTRACT:
Background: Atherosclerotic intracranial arterial stenosis represents a significant stroke cause increasingly treated with percutaneous transluminal angioplasty and stenting (PTAS), though this approach had not been compared to medical management in randomized trials.

Methods: Researchers randomly assigned patients experiencing recent transient ischemic attack or stroke from 70-99% diameter stenosis of major intracranial arteries to either aggressive medical management alone or combined with PTAS using the Wingspan stent system. The primary endpoint measured "stroke or death within 30 days after enrollment or after a revascularization procedure" plus stroke in the qualifying artery territory beyond 30 days.

RESULTS:
The trial was terminated early after 451 patient randomizations due to unfavorable outcomes in the PTAS group:
- 30-day rates: PTAS showed "14.7% stroke or death" (12.5% nonfatal, 2.2% fatal) compared to "5.8% in the medical-management group"
- Extended follow-up: Beyond 30 days, stroke occurrence was similar between groups (13 patients each)
- One-year outcomes: PTAS group experienced "20.0%" primary endpoint events versus "12.2% in the medical-management group"

Conclusion: Aggressive medical therapy proved superior to PTAS with the Wingspan stent, primarily due to heightened early stroke risk from the procedural intervention.

data.Press Release Title: FDA Approves Label Changes for Stryker Wingspan Stent System — Stenosis Threshold Raised from 50% to 70%
data.Press Release Content:
# FDA Revises Wingspan Stent System Indications

**Announcement Date:** August 8, 2012

**Device:** Wingspan intracranial stent system (Stryker Corporation)

**Key Changes:**
The FDA approved revised labeling for the Wingspan system, including narrowed indications for use, new contraindications and warnings, and a postmarket surveillance study requirement.

**Revised Indication:**
The device is now limited to patients with recurrent stroke despite medical management who have not experienced new stroke symptoms within 7 days before treatment.

**Clinical Data:**
The SAMMPRIS trial suggested the Wingspan system may present greater risks—including stroke and death—compared to medical management alone in some patients. However, differences exist between SAMMPRIS participants and those in the original humanitarian device exemption study that influence benefit-risk calculations.

**Regulatory Context:**
- Originally approved via Humanitarian Device Exemption in 2005
- Requires careful patient selection
- Device remains available for this specific patient subgroup who have exhausted other options

**Key Quote:**
"Patient benefit is an important factor in agency decision making," stated Dr. Jeffrey Shuren, FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health Director.

**Background:** Stryker acquired this neurovascular technology from Boston Scientific in January 2011.

data.Press Release Title: Stryker Broadway System Receives FDA Clearance
data.Press Release Content:
# Broadway System Summary

## Overview
The Broadway System is a fully integrated stroke access system designed for neurovascular intervention, specifically engineered to optimize access from the groin to the M1 (middle cerebral artery).

## Device Components

**Broadway 8 Catheter**
- Inner diameter: 0.084 inches (2.13mm)
- Distal outer diameter: 0.095 inches (2.41mm)
- Length: 132cm
- Features 36OMax Laser Technology with thousands of laser cuts in stainless steel hypotube

**Dash Hydrophilic Short Sheath**
- Inner diameter: 0.126 inches (3.20mm)
- Length: 11cm
- Designed as thin-wall, braided-reinforcement sheath with 8F closure compatibility

**Echo Intracranial Base Catheter**
- Inner diameter: 0.100 inches (2.54mm)
- Outer diameter: 0.118 inches (3.00mm)
- Lengths: 100cm and 105cm
- Features 36OMax laser technology

**FastPass Delivery Assist Catheter**
- Inner diameter: 0.0165 inches (0.42mm)
- Length: 156cm
- Minimizes ledge for smooth navigation

## Key Clinical Features
- Broadway 8 catheter's low profile closely matches average M1 diameter
- Offers 44% larger lumen compared to 0.070" ID catheters while maintaining handling characteristics
- Maintains lumen patency, kink resistance, and 8F closure compatibility

## Audience
Intended for healthcare professionals only.

data.Press Release Title: Stryker Completes Acquisition of Inari Medical, Inc. — Entry into Peripheral Vascular Clot Removal
data.Press Release Content:
# Stryker Acquisition of Inari Medical - Key Details

**Announcement Summary**
Stryker completed its acquisition of Inari Medical, Inc., a company specializing in venous thromboembolism (VTE) clot removal solutions. This move marks Stryker's entry into the peripheral vascular segment.

**Date**
February 19, 2025

**Company Details**
- **Acquirer:** Stryker Corporation (NYSE: SYK)
- **Target:** Inari Medical, Inc. (NASDAQ: NARI)
- **Focus:** Innovative VTE clot removal without thrombolytic drugs

**Key Quote**
Kevin Lobo, Stryker's Chair and CEO, stated: "The acquisition of Inari Medical marks a significant milestone in expanding our interventional endovascular portfolio."

**Strategic Importance**
The acquisition positions Stryker in the fast-growing VTE segment and expands its interventional endovascular capabilities. Stryker emphasized welcoming Inari's team to enhance patient outcomes globally.

**Clinical Data & FDA Information**
None provided in the announcement.

**Additional Resources**
The full press release is available through Stryker's investor relations website.

data.Press Release Title: Stryker Unveils New Intracranial Base Catheter to Streamline Neurovascular Access and Therapy Delivery
data.Press Release Content:
# Stryker AXS Lift Intracranial Base Catheter Announcement

**Announcement Date:** June 23, 2025

**What It Is:**
Stryker launched the AXS Lift Intracranial Base Catheter, designed to simplify neurovascular access during interventional procedures. The device provides both access and stable support from the groin puncture site to the cavernous segment of the internal carotid artery (ICA).

**Key Device Details:**
- Single catheter capable of providing reach and reliable support, potentially replacing the need for multiple catheters
- Features a large lumen to accommodate various therapies and techniques
- Addresses the clinical challenge of balancing trackability and support during neurointerventional procedures

**Clinical Context:**
The device was developed in collaboration with leading physicians to streamline access to the brain—a critical step in neurointerventional procedures.

**Key Quote:**
"With the AXS Lift, we set out to address a common challenge in neurovascular access by offering a single device capable of providing both reach and reliable support," said John Daniel, vice president of R&D at Stryker.

**Regulatory Status:**
Marked as "RX Only" (prescription required). Important safety information available via product documentation link provided.

**FDA/Regulatory Info:**
None mentioned in this announcement.

data.Press Release Title: WVU RNI Performs First In-Human Stroke Treatment Using New Broadway Catheter System
data.Press Release Content:
# WVU RNI First-in-Human Stroke Treatment Announcement

## Overview
West Virginia University's Rockefeller Neuroscience Institute (RNI) performed the first in-human procedure using Stryker Neurovascular's Broadway System for treating ischemic strokes caused by blood clots.

## Device Details
The **Broadway System** includes:
- Broadway 8 Catheter with 2.49mm outside diameter
- FastPass Delivery Assist Catheter
- Uses aspiration thrombectomy technology to remove clots via suction

Dr. SoHyun Boo, medical director of Neurointerventional Surgery, explained the engineering challenge: balancing flexibility to navigate blood vessel turns with sufficient rigidity to maintain suction without collapsing.

## Clinical Application
The procedure, called **endovascular thrombectomy**, was performed on a patient with tandem occlusion—blockages in both the carotid artery and middle cerebral artery. RNI treats approximately 120 ischemic strokes annually at Ruby Memorial.

## Regulatory Status
- FDA approval: December 2024
- RNI conducted cadaver testing with Stryker's R&D team before human use

## Key Quote
"Time is Brain" remains critical in stroke care: "Every minute the brain is not getting blood flow, about 2 million neurons are lost."

## Date
Procedure performed: February 2025

data.Press Release Title: Stryker's WEAVE Trial Demonstrates Positive Results — 2.6% Stroke/Death Rate in On-Label Use
data.Press Release Content: Stryker announced positive results from the WEAVE Trial (Wingspan StEnt System Post MArket SurVEillance Study), an FDA-mandated post-market surveillance study evaluating endovascular treatment for intracranial atherosclerotic disease (ICAD). Clinical Data: 2.6% observed stroke/death rate within 72 hours post-procedure vs 4.0% pre-specified success threshold and 9.7% null hypothesis rate. Device: Wingspan Stent System self-expanding Nitinol stent with Gateway PTA Balloon Catheter for treating ICAD. Approved under HDE; CE Mark in 2005. Regulatory: Limited to ages 22-80, 70-99% stenosis, Modified Rankin Score ≤3, ≥2 prior strokes refractory to medical therapy. Date: January 26, 2018 presentation at International Stroke Conference. Key Quote: Dr. Michael Alexander stated these results have potential to change stroke treatment in the future.

data.Press Release Title: WOVEN Trial: Wingspan One-Year Vascular Events and Neurologic Outcomes — 8.5% Stroke/Death at 1 Year. Published in J NeuroInterventional Surgery.
data.Press Release Content: WOVEN trial assessed one-year follow-up outcomes for patients treated with Wingspan stent for symptomatic intracranial atherosclerotic disease. Device: Wingspan stent by Stryker Neurovascular. Population: 129 patients from original WEAVE trial (152 initially enrolled across 24 sites). Key Findings: 2.6% periprocedural stroke/death rate; at one-year: seven additional strokes occurred (six minor, one major), zero deaths in delayed follow-up, combined outcome of 11 strokes/deaths among 129 patients (8.5% at one year). Conclusion: Relatively low 8.5% 1-year stroke/death rate in stented patients treated according to on-label protocols. Published April 2021 in Journal of NeuroInterventional Surgery.

data.Press Release Title: Inari Medical, Now Part of Stryker, Launches Artix Thrombectomy System — Combined Aspiration Plus Mechanical Thrombectomy for Arterial Applications
data.Press Release Content: Inari Medical (now part of Stryker) launched Artix Thrombectomy System on March 6, 2025, marking entry into arterial thrombectomy market for peripheral artery blood clots. Device: Combined aspiration plus mechanical thrombectomy system with over-the-wire design, 8 Fr. low-profile aspiration-capable sheath (65cm and 90cm lengths), mechanical element for acute-to-chronic clot removal, covered nitinol mesh funnel to restrict blood flow and minimize clot migration. First commercial case: October 19, 2024. Key Quote: Dr. Jonathan Bowman stated Artix marks significant advancement in peripheral arterial thromboembolism treatment.

data.Press Release Title: WEAVE Trial Final Results in 152 On-Label Patients — 2.6% Periprocedural Stroke/Death Rate. Published in Stroke.
data.Press Release Content: WEAVE trial postmarket surveillance study (FDA-mandated) assessed safety of Wingspan Stent System for treating symptomatic intracranial atherosclerotic disease. Population: 152 consecutive on-label patients across 24 hospitals (prospective, multicenter). On-Label Criteria: Ages 22-80, 70-99% intracranial atherosclerotic stenosis, Modified Rankin Scale ≤3, ≥2 prior strokes in affected vascular territory despite medical therapy, stenting ≥8 days after final stroke. Key Findings: Trial stopped early after interim analysis; 2.6% primary outcome (72-hour periprocedural events), 97.4% event-free rate, 1.3% nonfatal strokes, 1.3% deaths. Lowest reported complication rate in largest on-label Wingspan trial. Published April 2019 in Stroke.
