Conclusion: The Neuroform Atlas stent combined with coiling demonstrated high rates of complete aneurysm occlusion at one year with excellent safety and technical success profiles.

--- [3] WEAVE Trial: Final Results in 152 On-Label Patients (Stroke, 2019) ---
PMID 32561658 - WEAVE Trial (Final Results)

ABSTRACT:
The WOVEN study assessed one-year follow-up outcomes from 129 patients treated with Wingspan stents for symptomatic intracranial atherosclerotic disease according to on-label indications. The original WEAVE trial documented "a 2.6% periprocedural stroke and death rate" across 152 patients at 24 sites.

RESULTS:
The one-year follow-up revealed seven delayed strokes in the cohort—six minor and one major. Importantly, "no deaths were recorded in the cohort" during the delayed period beyond the periprocedural phase.

Combined with four periprocedural events from the initial WEAVE study, the cumulative outcome was "11 strokes or deaths of the 129 patients (8.5%) at the 1-year follow-up."

Conclusion: The investigators concluded this demonstrated "a relatively low 8.5% 1-year stroke and death rate in stented patients," noting the cohort provided a more homogeneous patient population than prior studies examining Wingspan stent efficacy.

--- [4] The WOVEN trial: Wingspan One-year Vascular Events and Neurologic Outcomes (Neurosurgery, 2020) ---
PMID 21899409 - SAMMPRIS Trial (NEJM 2011, Wingspan)

ABSTRACT:
Background: Atherosclerotic intracranial arterial stenosis represents a significant stroke cause increasingly treated with percutaneous transluminal angioplasty and stenting (PTAS), though this approach had not been compared to medical management in randomized trials.

Methods: Researchers randomly assigned patients experiencing recent transient ischemic attack or stroke from 70-99% diameter stenosis of major intracranial arteries to either aggressive medical management alone or combined with PTAS using the Wingspan stent system. The primary endpoint measured "stroke or death within 30 days after enrollment or after a revascularization procedure" plus stroke in the qualifying artery territory beyond 30 days.

RESULTS:
The trial was terminated early after 451 patient randomizations due to unfavorable outcomes in the PTAS group:
- 30-day rates: PTAS showed "14.7% stroke or death" (12.5% nonfatal, 2.2% fatal) compared to "5.8% in the medical-management group"
- Extended follow-up: Beyond 30 days, stroke occurrence was similar between groups (13 patients each)
- One-year outcomes: PTAS group experienced "20.0%" primary endpoint events versus "12.2% in the medical-management group"

Conclusion: Aggressive medical therapy proved superior to PTAS with the Wingspan stent, primarily due to heightened early stroke risk from the procedural intervention.

data.Press Release Title: SAMMPRIS Final Results: Aggressive Medical Treatment With or Without Stenting — Long-Term Follow-Up. Published in The Lancet.
data.Press Release Content: SAMMPRIS randomized controlled trial evaluated aggressive medical management vs stenting for high-risk intracranial artery stenosis patients (70-99% stenosis). Device: Wingspan stent system. Population: 451 patients with recent TIA or stroke (227 medical group, 224 stenting group). Follow-up: Median 32.4 months. Key Findings: Medical group superior outcomes - 15% vs 23% primary endpoint events; medical group lower cumulative probability of stroke/death (p=0.0252); stenting group higher rates of any stroke (26% vs 19%) and major hemorrhage (13% vs 4%). Conclusion: Aggressive medical management alone proved superior to PTAS with Wingspan stenting. Published The Lancet.

data.Press Release Title: Stryker Completes Acquisition of NICO Corporation — Expanding Minimally Invasive Neurosurgery
data.Press Release Content: Stryker completed acquisition of NICO Corporation (September 20, 2024), private company specializing in minimally invasive brain tumor removal and intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) treatment solutions. Device: BrainPath and Myriad products enable minimally invasive approaches for ICH procedures. Clinical Data: ICH affects over 3.4 million annually worldwide; mortality 40-50% within 30 days; NICO products enable treatment with improved functional outcomes compared to guideline-based medical management alone. Context: ICH deadliest form of stroke with rising incidence due to aging populations. Key Quote: Andy Pierce stated this acquisition enables differentiated, minimally invasive approaches driving meaningful outcomes.

data.Press Release Title: SAMMPRIS Trial: Aggressive Medical Management Superior to PTAS with Wingspan — Trial Stopped Early. Published in NEJM.
data.Press Release Content:
SAMMPRIS Trial - Stenting Versus Aggressive Medical Therapy for Intracranial Arterial Stenosis
Published: New England Journal of Medicine, September 15, 2011
Primary Publication: NEJM 2011;365:993-1003
DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa1105335

Study Leadership:
- Primary Investigator: Marc I. Chimowitz, MD
- Multi-institutional collaboration
- Funded by National Institutes of Health

Trial Design and Patient Population:

Target Disease:
- Symptomatic intracranial arterial stenosis
- Stenosis severity: 70-99% in major intracranial arteries
- Patient status: Recent TIA or stroke within 30 days prior to enrollment
- Population at high risk: Recurrent events despite medical therapy

Total Enrollment:
- 451 patients randomized (trial terminated early for safety)
- Primary vessels affected: Intracranial arteries (ICA, vertebral, basilar, major branches)

Randomization Groups:

Group 1 - PTAS (Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty and Stenting):
- Aggressive medical therapy PLUS endovascular intervention
- Device: Wingspan stent system (Boston Scientific)
- Procedure: Angioplasty followed by stent placement
- Additional treatment: Full medical optimization

Group 2 - Aggressive Medical Management Alone:
- No endovascular intervention
- Medications: Aspirin, clopidogrel (dual antiplatelet therapy)
- Antihypertensive agents (goal <140/90 mmHg)
- Lipid-lowering therapy (statins)
- Intensive lifestyle modifications
- Risk factor management

Key Findings - Periprocedural Safety (30 Days):

PTAS Group (Stenting):
- Stroke or death rate: 14.7% within 30 days
- Most events: Nonfatal strokes (approximately 12%)
- Death rate: Lower but significant (~2.7%)
- Complications: Primarily ischemic strokes during/after procedure

Medical Management Group:
- Stroke or death rate: 5.8% within 30 days
- Significantly lower than PTAS group
- Absolute difference: 8.9 percentage points
- Risk ratio: 2.5-fold increased risk with PTAS

One-Year Outcomes (Primary Endpoint):

PTAS Group:
- Primary endpoint (stroke or death at 1 year): 20.0%
- Cumulative event rate higher over time
- Both early and late complications contributed

Medical Management Group:
- Primary endpoint (stroke or death at 1 year): 12.2%
- Approximately 8% absolute difference maintained
- Risk stratification: PTAS group consistently higher risk

Clinical Interpretation of Results:

Why PTAS Failed:
1. HIGH PERIPROCEDURAL RISK: 14.7% 30-day stroke/death rate unacceptably high
2. PROCEDURAL COMPLICATIONS: Technical difficulty of intracranial intervention
3. VESSEL TORTUOSITY: Challenging anatomy in intracranial circulation
4. STENT COMPLICATIONS: Thrombosis, restenosis, vessel dissection
5. MEDICAL MANAGEMENT SUPERIOR: Better outcomes with medications alone

Why Medical Management Succeeded:
1. LOWER IMMEDIATE RISK: 5.8% early event rate manageable
2. MEDICAL OPTIMIZATION: Effective at preventing recurrent events
3. NO PROCEDURAL COMPLICATIONS: Avoided iatrogenic stroke risk
4. MEDICATION EFFICACY: Dual antiplatelet therapy and other drugs effective
5. LOWER OVERALL MORTALITY: Fewer deaths in medical group

Trial Stopping Rationale:

Independent Data Safety Monitoring Board Decision:
- Recommended halting enrollment April 5, 2011
- Reasons:
  1. Clear safety advantage for medical management
  2. Futility analysis showed minimal chance PTAS would become superior
  3. Ethical obligation to avoid randomizing further patients to higher-risk PTAS
  4. Pre-planned interim analysis triggered termination

Implications for Intracranial Stenting:

1. Wingspan Stent Efficacy Issues:
   - Device itself may be problematic
   - Technical challenges in deployment
   - High restenosis rates
   - Difficult to achieve optimal positioning

2. Procedural Complications:
   - Intracranial intervention inherently risky
   - Vessel perforation possible
   - Dissection from guidewires/balloons
   - Thromboembolic complications from manipulation

3. Medical Therapy Progress:
   - Modern antithrombotic regimens highly effective
   - Statins reduce event risk
   - Blood pressure control important
   - Lifestyle modifications contribute

Clinical Practice Impact:

Before SAMMPRIS:
- Intracranial stenting gaining popularity
- Wingspan approved for HDE (humanitarian use) in 2005
- Off-label use of coronary stents common
- Expectation: Stenting would improve outcomes vs. medical therapy

After SAMMPRIS:
- Dramatically reduced intracranial stenting
- Medical management became standard of care
- Only selected, high-risk recurrent cases considered for intervention
- Renewed focus on optimizing medical therapy
- FDA issued restrictions on Wingspan use

Guideline Recommendations Post-SAMMPRIS:

AHA/ASA Stroke Guidelines:
- Intracranial stenting NOT recommended for symptomatic stenosis (Class III evidence)
- Aggressive medical management IS recommended (Class I evidence)
- Angioplasty/stenting reserved for specific cases with very high recurrence risk
- Wingspan use dramatically limited

Patient Selection After SAMMPRIS:
- Very high-risk patients: Recurrent events despite optimal medical therapy
- Specific vessels: Basilar artery with recurrence might be considered
- Not first-line: Reserved for failed medical management
- Careful case selection required

Long-Term Implications:

Research Direction Shift:
- Moved away from intracranial stenting development
- Focused on improving medical therapies
- Investigation of other endovascular techniques
- Better understanding of stenosis pathophysiology

Device Development Impact:
- Wingspan sales declined significantly
- Limited further device development for intracranial stenting
- Attention shifted to acute stroke thrombectomy devices
- Boston Scientific later sold Wingspan to Stryker

Publication Statistics:
- Highly cited landmark trial (thousands of citations)
- Considered definitive evidence against routine intracranial stenting
- Influenced global practice patterns
- Changed interventional neuroradiology field

Study Significance:
The SAMMPRIS trial provided class I evidence that aggressive medical management was superior to stenting with the Wingspan stent for symptomatic intracranial arterial stenosis, establishing medical therapy as the standard of care and significantly limiting the role of percutaneous angioplasty and stenting in this patient population. The trial's strong negative result for intervention fundamentally changed clinical practice and remains one of the most important negative intervention trials in stroke medicine.

=== KEY FEATURES ===
=== KEY FEATURES ===

The Paragon 8F Balloon Guide Catheter is designed for use in facilitating the insertion and guidance of an intravascular catheter into a selected blood vessel in the peripheral and neurovascular systems. The balloon provides temporary vascular occlusion during angiographic procedures, and is also indicated for use as a conduit for retrieval devices.
=== KEY FEATURES ===

Indicated for use in facilitating the insertion and guidance of an intravascular catheter into a selected blood vessel in the peripheral and neurovascular systems. The balloon provides temporary vascular occlusion during these and other angiographic procedures, and is also indicated for use as a conduit for retrieval devices.

The pRESET Thrombectomy Device is a minimally invasive stent retriever used to remove blood clots that cause acute ischemic strokes. It features a helical slit and closed proximal ring design for effective thrombus removal in proximal and distal vessels.

- Enables improved engagement with thrombus material
- Provides structural support and control

data.Press Release Title: Phenox Announces Distribution Agreement with Wallaby Medical for Avenir Detachable Coil System (US and EU)
data.Press Release Content: phenox secured exclusive distribution agreement with Wallaby Medical for Avenir Detachable Coil System in US and EU markets (September 23, 2019). Device: Avenir intended for endovascular embolization of intracranial aneurysms, arteriovenous malformations (AVMs), arteriovenous fistulae, and peripheral arterial/venous embolization. Features: Comprehensive coil line with stable framing coils, extra volume filling coils, ultra-soft finishing coils; integrated mechanical detachment (no external energy required), anatomically adaptive pusher technology, patent-pending elastic nitinol lasso detachment zone technology. Clinical: Successfully treated over 100 patients in USA, China, Latin America. Regulatory: Expected official launch USA/Europe later 2019. Expert Quote: Professor Youxiang Li praised Avenir coils for smooth delivery and exceptional visibility.

data.Press Release Title: Wallaby Acquires German Neurovascular Leader Phenox to Accelerate Global Expansion
data.Press Release Content: Wallaby Medical announced acquisition of phenox GmbH (including femtos GmbH) for approximately EUR 500 million including milestone payments; deal closed April 21, 2022. Companies: Wallaby (Laguna Hills, CA; Shanghai, China) acquired phenox (Bochum, Germany). Wallaby Portfolio: Avenir Coil System (intracranial aneurysm), Esperance Aspiration Catheter (ischemic stroke; US/China clearance), Esperance Distal Access Catheter (China). phenox Portfolio: p64/p48 flow diverter range, pRESET stent retriever range (mechanical thrombectomy), sold in 45+ countries. Leadership: Michael Alper remains CEO; Prof. Dr.-Ing. Hermann Monstadt (phenox founder) becomes Managing Director. Key Quote: Alper stated Wallaby-phenox combination will have deep roots in Europe/China, strong US presence, recent Japan market entrance.

data.Press Release Title: Phenox (Wallaby Medical) Announces FDA 510(k) Clearance of pRESET Thrombectomy Device for Acute Ischemic Stroke
data.Press Release Content: phenox Inc. (Wallaby Medical company) received FDA 510(k) clearance for pRESET Thrombectomy Device to treat acute ischemic stroke in United States (announced February 6, 2023). Device: Purpose: Minimally invasive blood clot removal for acute ischemic stroke; easy to use with quick deployment. Indication: Reduces disability in patients with persistent proximal anterior circulation large vessel occlusion and smaller core infarcts who received thrombolytic therapy first. Market: Available in Europe for over 10 years; now US approved. Position: phenox only 3rd company in US to receive this device indication. Key Quote: Andrew Cormack (CCO) stated pRESET has been used successfully in Europe over a decade and will be well-received by US physicians. Market: Global AIS market projected to exceed $2 billion by 2025 with 5.12% CAGR.

data.Article Abstracts & Results:
--- [1] Thrombectomy With the pRESET vs Solitaire Stent Retrievers as First-Line Large V (JAMA Neurology, 2024) ---
Title: Tenzing 7 delivery catheter with or without a leading microwire for single pass aspiration thrombectomy: A multicenter experience. The study examines aspiration mechanical thrombectomy using the Tenzing 7 (T7) delivery catheter. Traditionally, these procedures require an inner microcatheter and leading microwire; however, early experience suggests the microwire may be unnecessary with T7's soft tapered tip design. The researchers conducted a retrospective review of consecutive patients undergoing single-pass thrombectomy with T7 across six institutions (2020-2022), analyzing successful aspiration catheter delivery, procedural time, and complication rates. Results showed that among 89 patients, 19 (21%) used a leading microwire while 70 (79%) did not. Successful AC delivery was similar with and without microwires (97% vs. 90%, p = 0.15). Median puncture-to-revascularization times were comparable (17 minutes with microwire vs. 16 minutes without, p = 0.12). One minor complication occurred (T7-related vasospasm) in the no-microwire group, with no statistically significant difference in complication rates. The conclusion suggests that initial passes with T7 can be performed without a leading microwire, reserving microwire use for difficult cases.

--- [2] pRESET thrombectomy device outcomes in patients with acute ischemic stroke: A sy (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2024) ---
Study Design: This was a multicenter, randomized, controlled, noninferiority trial comparing two stent retriever devices for treating large vessel occlusion strokes. Key Findings: Among 340 enrolled patients, the pRESET stent retriever achieved functional independence in 54.9% of cases versus 57.5% for the Solitaire retriever at 90 days. The difference of -2.57% fell within the noninferiority margin, establishing that pRESET retriever was noninferior to the Solitaire retriever. Safety Profile: Symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage occurred in no pRESET patients compared to 1.2% in the Solitaire group. Mortality rates were similar between groups at approximately 14.5%. Angiographic Outcomes: Both devices demonstrated comparable effectiveness in achieving complete reperfusion. The pRESET group achieved target perfusion scores in 84.4% of cases versus 89.2% for Solitaire. Conclusion: The researchers determined that pRESET offers a safe and effective option for flow restoration and disability reduction in patients with LVO stroke, providing an additional treatment alternative for acute stroke intervention.

--- [3] Use of the pRESET LITE thrombectomy device in combined approach for medium vesse (Journal of Neurointerventional Surgery, 2024) ---
STRATIS registry anterior circulation complete reperfusion analysis - Study analyzed 563 patients from the STRATIS registry who achieved complete or near-complete reperfusion (eTICI 2c/3) for anterior circulation strokes via endovascular thrombectomy. RESULTS: Multipass Reperfusion (n=186): 'increased time to treatment was associated with a decreased likelihood of a good clinical outcome' with odds ratio of 0.71 per 60-minute delay (P=0.005). First-Pass Reperfusion (n=377): 'no association between increased time to treatment and good clinical outcomes was observed' with odds ratio of 0.93 per 60-minute delay (P=0.347). Primary Conclusion: 'First-pass reperfusion may compensate for the effects of delays to reperfusion on functional outcomes in ischemic stroke.' This suggests achieving complete reperfusion in a single device pass may mitigate the typical time-dependent decline in stroke outcomes, distinguishing it from cases requiring multiple passes.

--- [4] Stent Retriever Thrombectomy of Small Caliber Intracranial Vessels Using pREset  (Journal of Neurosurgery, 2016) ---
Surpass Evolve flow diverter latest data - Search yielded 37 total results spanning 2016-2026, with most recent publications (2025: 12 results, 2024: 10 results). Key Findings: 2025 meta-analysis examined 'cumulative evidence on the safety and efficacy' of these devices in treating intracranial aneurysms. 2024 comprehensive review compared Surpass Streamline and Evolve devices, noting 'mortality, complications and in-stent thrombosis were significantly reduced with Evolve'. One study of 116 patients (2024) evaluated safety and efficacy across both ruptured and unruptured aneurysms. Full abstracts and results sections not available.
